question: Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, what range of melting points has been reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides?
answer:
Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, the following range of melting points has been reported:

- ZrB2 has a melting point of 3245 °C [7].
- HfB2 has a melting point of 3380 °C [23].
- ZrC has a melting point of 3530 °C [10].
- HfC has a melting point of 3900 °C [24].
- The oxidation product ZrO2 has a melting point of 2700 °C [14].
- The oxidation product HfO2 has a melting point of 2810 °C [1].
- Ta2O5, another oxidation product, has a melting point of 1800 °C [1].

Thus, the range of melting points reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides spans from 1800 °C to 3900 °C.

refractory carbides, nitrides and borides of Hf, Zr and Ta, etc, which are classified as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have remarkably raised owing to the striking properties, like supernal melting points, high excellent thermal conductivity, moderate coefficient of thermal expansion and high strength and modulus [4 e 7]. Refractory carbides including HfC (3890 /C14 C), TaC (3880 /C14 C) and ZrC (3540 /C14 C) with the highest melting points among UHTCs were reported to have lower vapor pressures than those of nitrides and borides, which were considered promising candidate materials for TPS [8 e 11]. The corresponding solid oxidation products, HfO2 (2810 /C14 C) and ZrO2 (2715 /C14 C) have higher melting points than that of Ta2O5 (1800 /C14 C), which could be more stable in environments above 2000 /C14 C [12]. However, the implementation of pure HfC and ZrC is restricted due to the weak oxidation inhibition at intermediate temperatures, forming porous oxide scale and making it susceptible to the ablative environment [13,14].

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
The sintering temperature range for densification of ZrB2 is influenced by melting point of the silicides used as the sinter aids [34,134,136,137]. The densification temperature (1600 °C) is lower for ZrSi2-reinforced ZrB2 when compared to other silicides since the melting point of ZrSi2 is relatively low. Sciti et al. [112,134,149] extensively studied the use of MoSi2 sinter-additive for ZrB2/ HfB2. Their work revealed that achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density was possible for ZrB2 with the use of MoSi2 (3-20 vol %) via PS at 1900 °C, HP at 1800 °C and SPS at 1750 °C. The densification was assisted by transient liquid phase that formed due to the reaction between MoSi2 and surface oxide impurities on ZrB2, as was shown by Eq. (30). During hot pressing, the plastic deformation of silicides at high temperatures (> 800 °C) would also contributes to the densification of ZrB2 as the deformation forces soft silicide particles to fill in the voids left by ZrB2 particles [112,134].

2. Crystal structure, bonding and phase diagram of transition metal borides
Both HfB and HfB2 phases have homogeneity range and HfB2 has the highest melting point (3380 °C) [57,59]. In Ta-B phase diagram, there are six boride phases, such as Ta2B, Ta3B2, TaB, Ta3B4, TaB2 and Ta5B6 [60]. Among these phases, only TaB and TaB2 are stable from room temperature (RT) up to their melting temperatures. The TaB2 phase, possessing a melting temperature of ~3200 °C, has a wide compositional range between 61 and 72 at.% B or 64 and 72 at.% B below the eutectic temperature of ~2055 °C [60]. INTRODUCTION
With laser pulse heating (heating time about 1 s), the  authors  of  [4]  found  that  the  congruent  melting temperature of TaC Tm = 4277 ± 12 K is higher than the melting point of HfC Tm =  4250  ±  20  K.  From other studies [5-7] with rapid microsecond heating, it follows  that  the  most  refractory  is  mixed  carbide Ta0.8 Hf0.2 C with a melting point 4300 ± 80 K [7]. For HfC and TaC in [5, 6], the literature data on the radiative characteristics at temperatures below the melting point  were  used.  A  consequence  of  this  was  an increased  temperature  measurement  error,  approximately equal to the difference in the melting temperatures of the mentioned carbides.
In theoretical study [8], the question of substances with maximum melting temperatures was considered and it was predicted that the maximum melting temperature, exceeding all known ones, will be that of hafnium carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.38 . Review [9] provides a detailed analysis and comparison of the properties of HfC, HfN, and Hf(C,N); in accordance with [8], a conclusion is drawn that the carbonitride HfC 0.51 N0.38 may have a record high melting point 4321-4445 K. In stationary experiment [10], HfC carbide and hafnium  carbonitride  HfC 0.5 N0.35 were  heated  under identical  conditions,  which  yielded  the  melting  of HfC,  while  hafnium  carbonitride  remained  in  an unmelted state.
In this article, the physical properties of carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.32 are studied for the first time based on a fast pulse experiment (below, this carbonitride is denoted Hf(C,N)) at high temperatures in solid and liquid  states,  including  the  melting  region,  and  its melting point is estimated.

Introduction
Borides, carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals such as ZrB2, HfB2, HfC, or HfN belong to a class of ceramics defined ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with extremely high melting temperatures. These materials are of particular interest because of the unique property combination of high refractoriness, high-electrical and -thermal conductivity, chemical inertness against molten metals or nonbasic slags and good thermal shock resistance. 1,2 These properties make them attractive candidates for high-temperature applications where corrosion-wear-oxidation resistance are demanded. ZrB2 has several applications such as Hall-Heroult cell cathodes for electrochemical processing of aluminium, evaporation boats, crucibles for handling molten metals, thermowell tubes for steel refining, thermocouples sleeves for high-temperature uses, nozzles, plasma electrodes, or as dispersoid in metal and ceramic-matrix composites for heaters and igniters. In addition, ZrB 2 is a metallic conductor with electrical resistivity comparable with those of their parent metals. This permits to produce complex components at reduced shaping costs. HfB2, because of its higher costs of production, has found a limited number of applications, in wear-resistant coatings for instance. Similar to ZrB2, HfB2 has also been recently considered in ultra-high-temperature composites for aerospace applications (leading edge parts on re-entry vehicles), in conjuction with SiC, because of its excellent refractoriness. 3,4

1. Introduction
Hafnium diboride (HfB2) is a refractory metal boride that exhibits excellent  thermal  and  mechanical  properties,  including  high  melting point  (Tm~3380 ◦ C),  high  hardness  (~28  GPa),  and  good  oxidation resistance  [1 -4].  These  properties  make  it  a  promising  material  for extreme environments, such as protective tiles on nose cones and leading  edges  of  re-entry  vehicles.  Despite  the  promising  combination  of properties,  monolith  HfB2  has  limited  applications  owing  to  its  poor sinterability.  The  high  melting  point  and  strong  covalent  bonding  of HfB2 pose  challenges  to  its  sintering  process,  impeding  complete densification  and  leading  to  significant  grain  growth  during  conventional sintering (T > 0.5Tm) [3].
To address these challenges, various sintering techniques, such as hot pressing  (HP)  [1,5 -7],  pressure-less  sintering  (PS)  [8],  reactive  hot pressing (RHP) [9], self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10], and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [11 -18], have been employed to enhance  the  densification  of  HfB2-based  composites.  Overall,  these studies demonstrate that SPS is an effective technique for consolidating HfB2 ceramics with high relative density (up to ~ 98 %) and homogeneous  microstructure  [3,4,15 -20]. 3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
Although the melting point of ZrC (3540 °C) is close to the sublimation temperature of carbon (3550 °C), it has a much lower density of 6.6 g/cm 3 than TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) and HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ), and is cheaper. Besides, its oxidation-resistant property and high strength and stiffness can also effectively improve the operating performance of C/C in SRM. Shen et al. [68] prepared C/C-ZrC composites by an in-situ reaction process as mentioned above, using ZrOCl2 · 8H2O as raw material, and investigated their ablation behaviors in a small SRM hot-fire testing system. The as-prepared composites were exposed to the combustion gases for 3-4 s at a temperature of 3361-3386 K and a chamber pressure of 10.5-15.8 MPa, as well as to the oxyacetylene flame (about 3273 K for 60 s) for a comparison [98] . During the ablation of the composites, the preferential oxidation of ZrC reduced the carbon consumption due to the lower Gibbs free energy for the heterogeneous reactions between ZrC and oxygen-containing species. The melting of solid ZrO2 and the evaporation of liquid ZrO2 absorbed heat from the combustion gases, which relieved the erosive attack on the carbon. Consequently, the presence of ZrC improved the ablation resistance of C/C composites. However, the linear ablation rate in the SRM with highpressure and high-velocity combustion gases was much higher than that in the oxyacetylene flame. Lots of pits and ZrO2 particles, and cone shaped fibers or irregularly shaped carbon fragments, appeared on the ablation surface of C/C-ZrC composites in the SRM (Fig. 3). Different ablation rates and ablation appearance features were attributed to the different ablation mechanisms.

1. Introduction
Refractory transition metal borides and carbides have extremely high melting points of over 3000 ◦ C and hence are referred to as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Even though they have been studied since the 1960s, there has been recent interest in these materials as potential candidates for thermal protection systems on hypersonic vehicles. A developmental history of UHTC materials can be found in Opeka et al. 1 and they have recently been reviewed by Paul et al. 2 The initial selection of UHTC materials was based on their melting temperatures, however oxidation temperature and the melting points of their oxides is, in fact, more critical. There are a number of materials with melting points over 3000 ◦ C, whose oxides also have melting points in excess of 2500 ◦ C, for example ZrB2, HfB2 and HfC. These materials are widely studied for high temperature

applications as monolithic components, however single phase ceramics are significantly limited in this area as a result of their very poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance. 3 Even with the addition of a second or third ceramic phase such as SiC or LaB6, these materials do not possess the high temperature resistance, thermal shock resistance or fracture toughness because of the volatilisation and decomposition of the oxidation products. 4
The desired properties may require the development of fibre reinforced UHTC composites to enable viable application development beyond 2500 ◦ C. For such hybrid materials, carbon fibre is the preferred choice owing to its high strength, ready availability and ability to be formed into complex shapes, 5 provided it can be protected from oxidation.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

4. Discussion
La2O3 ( ∼ 2100 ◦ C) has a lower melting point than SiC ( ∼ 2800 ◦ C), ZrB2 ( ∼ 3200 ◦ C) and HfB2 ( ∼ 3400 ◦ C) which induces plastic deformation at lower temperatures. This has been observed in other ceramic systems such as Al2O3, which has a similar melting point ( ∼ 2100 ◦ C) to La2O3 and plastic deformation at 10 -3 s -1 in micron-sized Al2O3 could be obtained at 1400 ◦ C with stresses around 100 MPa. 51,52 Grain boundary chemistry is affected by thermal treatment which could be beneficial for the strength of UHTCs at high temperature. Hilmas and Fahrenholtz 53 reported an enhancement of up to 40% of the strength of HfB2-SiC ceramics at 1600 ◦ C using a thermal treatment in Ar of 10 h at 1550 ◦ C. This effect was attributed to a grain boundary chemistry change during the thermal treatment. Further investigation of this effect and strength data at higher temperatures is required to understand high temperature mechanical response of UHTCs. However, this field is challenging since some phenomena such as creep, have only been explored very recently, 12 and its effect on UHTCs properties at working temperature is unknown.

1. Introduction
To protect C/C composite in extremely high-temperature conditions, the protective coating layers having heat-resistant and anti-oxidative properties  are  required.  Among  the  protective  materials,  ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  particularly  noteworthy  due  to their extremely high melting temperatures around 3000 ◦ C or higher, exceptional hardness, as well as excellent chemical stability and strength under  high-temperature  conditions  [5,6].  Among  UHTCs,  zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a remarkably high melting point of 3530 ◦ C [7,8]. Additionally, its oxide, ZrO2, also has a high melting point of 2700 ◦ C [9].  Furthermore,  ZrC  exhibits  excellent  resistance  to  corrosion  and possesses a density of 6.73 g/cm 3 [10], which is notably lower compared to other UHTCs such as HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ) [11], TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) [12], and WC (15.8 g/cm 3 ) [13].
To coat ZrC layer on the C/C composite, it is crucial to utilize the corresponding ZrC powders in the VPS method. Consequently, it becomes inevitable to consider the effects of the particle size of the raw powders on the quality of the coating layer. For example, when the particle size increases, it is generally anticipated to achieve a higher coating rate but there is a possibility of surface damage due to the impact of un-melted powders. The heat of melting of Hf(C,N) 0.9 kJ/g is approximately the same as that of HfC. The heat capacities of these two substances, far from the melting region, separately in the solid and separately in the liquid phases, are practically  the  same. 1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].

KEYWORDS
Developing novel ultra-high-melting-point UHTCs with enhanced elevated-temperature mechanical performance and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance is imperative to address the critical thermal barrier in hypersonic vehicle TPS. 7,8 Hafnium carbonitride (HfCN) ceramics, with a melting point of up to 4419 ± 37 K, have become the material with the highest melting point among all discovered compounds, and have gradually received high attention in TPS in recent years. 9-11 Suvorova et al. 11 synthesized HfCN ceramics via spark plasma sintering (SPS), followed by evaluation of their mechanical properties and oxidation resistance at 1200 ◦ C. Zeng et al. 12 synthesized HfCN ceramic powder via hafnium nitride carbonization and consolidated it by SPS, demonstrating superior ablation resistance over commercial HfC. Meanwhile, Peng et al. 13 investigated the ablation/oxidation behavior of HfCN ceramics under ultra-high temperatures (3273 K), revealing a 90% reduction in linear ablation rate compared to monolithic HfC ceramics. In addition, Xu et al. 14 fabricated HfCN via hot-pressing sintering, assessing its thermomechanical properties from RT-2000 ◦ C under Ar and revealing significant C/N ratio dependence of mechanical properties in HfC x N 1-x ceramics. The existing studies have preliminarily shown that HfCN ceramics possess significant potential for application in TPS of hypersonic vehicles. However, most high-temperature mechanical properties research on HfCN materials has focused on argon or vacuum environments, and the hightemperature mechanical properties of HfCN ceramics in air environments lack systematic investigation, which is critical to the safety and application in TPS. Furthermore, current fabrication restricts bulk HfCN ceramics to diameters below 50 mm. Addressing these gaps requires, there is a pressing demand for the development of large-scale HfCN ceramics.

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Even though borides are more extensively used for the ultra-high temperature applications, some carbides, such as the mono-carbides of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb, are also classified as UHTCs. Unlike the borides, these carbides crystallize in cubic close packed structures. The more commonly used HfC, ZrC and TaC possess face centered cubic or the NaCl structure (Wuchina, 2007; Pierson, 1996; Pierrat, 2010) (Figure 2a). Usually the host metal atoms form cubic close packed structures with
Figure 1. (a) Crystal structure (P6/mmm) of the refractory di-borides [Fahrenholtz, 2007, Li, 2010] and (b) Zr-B phase diagram showing very narrow homogeneity range for ZrB 2 [McHale, 1994]
the  carbon  atoms  occupying  the  octahedral  interstitial  sites.  Compared  to  the  borides, still  higher  melting points have been reported for the carbides, with some solid solution carbides like Ta x Hf 1-x Cy possessing melting points in excess of 4000 o C (Cotton, 2010; Wuchina, 2007). However, unlike the borides, some issues related to their oxidation resistance (Wuchina, 2007) restrict their applications and this will
be discussed in the following section. In contrast to the M-B, the published M-C phase diagrams (Figure 2b) reveal presence of considerably wider homogeneity range, which implies presence of non-stoichiometry in their compositions and related defects in their lattices. Also, the presence of the defects such as carbon vacancies  allows  tailoring  of  the  thermo-physical  and  thermo-mechanical  properties (Wuchina, 2007; Pierrat, 2010; Wuchina, 2004).

Introduction
Important previous studies reported that MI of the Zr-Ti alloy is effective for forming ZrC and TiC dual phase matrices in porous carbon materials [25] and C/C preforms [26]. Zr-Ti alloys are all-proportional solid solutions, where the melting point of the alloys with Ti contents of 0-80 at% varies within the range of 1540-1700 /C176 C, which is higher than the melting point of Si and lower than the melting point of Zr [27]. The mechanical properties and heat resistance of Si-free Cf/UHTCMCs also overcome the heat resistance limitation determined by the evaporation temperature of B2O3 and SiO2. Previous studies showed that Zr-Ti alloy infiltration is effective for improving the heat resistance of C/UHTCMCs [26]. However, the applicability of these materials at extreme temperatures above 2000 /C176 C is unknown.
Thus, the objective of this study is to evaluate the potential utility of C/UHTCMCs with Zr-Ti alloy infiltration at temperatures of 2000 /C176 C or above. To evaluate the applicability of these materials, three types of C/UHTCMCs fabricated using different alloy compositions are subjected to arc-wind tunnel testing. The heat flux is set to below and above 2000 /C176 C. The microstructure is characterized, and the degradation behavior is examined based on thermodynamic analysis.

1. Introduction
In extreme environments, such as in the space and power industries, materials with high-temperature oxidation resistance, ultra-high melting point, and good thermodynamic stability are desirable. However, fewer than 20 materials have melting temperatures higher than 3000 ◦ C  [1].  Ultra-high  temperature  carbides  with  a  melting  point greater  than 3273 K have attracted much attention due to their low thermal  conductivity,  excellent  refractoriness,  high  hardness,  high elastic  modulus,  good  electrical  conductivity,  and  good  mechanical properties. TaC and HfC can form a continuous series of solid solutions with  a  face-centred  cubic  lattice  under  any  mixing  ratio.  In  1967, Andrievskii et al. [2] determined, using the Pirani-Alterthum method, that the melting point of 4TaC-1HfC was in the range of 3970 to 4010 ◦ C. In 2016, Cedillos-Barraza et al. [3,4] used pulsed laser heating to show

3.1. Phase analysis
The XRD pattern of HfB2 powder and SPS consolidated pellets from temperature 1750 -1950 ◦ C is shown in Fig. 1a . Notably, there is a slight peak shift of ~0.2 ◦ (as shown in Fig. 1b) towards left for H1800, H1850, H1900 and H1950, however, HfB2 phase is retained. This slight shift is attributed to presence of ~2 MPa tensile residual stresses (calculation provided  in  appendix  (equation  v  and  vi)  in  the  HfB ₂ phase  at  T ≥ 1800 ◦ C. During SPS cooling, differential contraction between the core and surface of HfB2 can induce tensile surface stresses, which is attributed to low thermal expansion (CTE~6.3 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6  1/ ◦ C) of HfB ₂ and rapid cooling  from  high  temperatures  [3].  Nevertheless,  the  XRD  pattern Fig.  1a  also  accentuates  the  presence  of  low-intensity  peaks  corresponding to the Hafnium Carbide (HfC) phase (~1 vol%), indicating its presence as an impurity in pristine powder as well as sintered pellets. The lattice parameters for hafnium diboride (HfB2) are determined to be a = b = 3.143 Å and c = 3.476 Å. In contrast, the lattice parameters for hafnium carbide (HfC) are, a = b = c = 4.637 Å. The presence of HfC was further confirmed by microstructural analysis using EDS as shown in Fig. 1c (the presence of HfC and HfB2  at all temperatures is shown in appendix, Fig. A3). The presence of HfC is ignored in studying the sintering kinetics in the present study.

3.4. Multiphase UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
For example, the melting point of metallic Zr is 1855 ◦ C, while Zr-Si can form two eutectic alloys with lower melting points-Zr9Si1 (melting point: 1570 ◦ C) and Zr1Si9 (melting point: 1370 ◦ C)-allowing for RMI processes at lower temperatures; (2) the thermal expansion coefficients of SiC are close to those of C/C, significantly lower than those of UHTCs and their oxides, preventing micro-cracks or damage to carbon fibers during composite preparation; (3) during ablation, SiO2 exhibits excellent fluidity, which helps compensate for defects caused by oxidation of UHTCs, addressing issues of oxide cracking and delamination from the matrix; and (4) the formation of SiO2-UHTCs oxide symbiotic structures or silicates further resists the erosion of flame jets and oxygen penetration, thus better protecting the C/C matrix [71,121].

1. Introduction
Generally,  Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  refer  to  ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ C, including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Known for their exceptionally high melting points, hardness, modulus and excellent high-temperature oxidation resistance, UHTCs have garnered significant attention in thermal protection applications [4]. Among them, HfC and TaC are extensively studied [5]. Ta and Hf, located in groups IVB and VB of  the  periodic  table,  have  similar  atomic  radii  due  to  lanthanide contraction, enabling unlimited mutual solubility to form Ta-Hf-C solid solutions [6]. Ta4HfC5  synthesized in a molar ratio of 4:1 of TaC:HfC exhibits  a  melting  point  approaching  4000 ◦ C  [7].  Due  to  the  high melting  point  and  superior  thermophysical  and  oxidation-resistant properties inherited from TaC and HfC, Ta4HfC5  holds promising applications in extreme high-temperature aerospace environments [8 -10].
To apply Ta4HfC5 in thermal protection materials, two fundamental

3.2. Oxidation behavior of the SiC/(Hf0.2Ta0.8)C nanocomposites
To reveal the intrinsic reasons for the excellent oxidation resistance of  SiC/(Hf0.2Ta0.8)C  nanocomposite  materials in  the  medium to  high temperature  range,  XRD  characterization  was  carried  out  on  the oxidized, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. It can be observed that within this temperature range, the characteristic peaks of SiO2  are the most prominent. In addition, oxides such as Ta2O5, and Hf6Ta2O17 can also be detected. Based on previous studies [20], it is speculated that the high melting-point phases in the oxide scales (e.g., Hf6Ta2O17) act as frameworks, while the low melting-point phases (e.g., glassy phase SiO2) act as melt-filling phases, which jointly form the structurally favored oxide  scales,  thereby  slowing  down  the  oxidation  rate.  Interestingly, non-stoichiometric  Ta2O5-x appears  at  oxidation  temperature  higher than  1400 ◦ C  [40].  Studies  have  shown  that  the  Ta-O  system  has  a broadened  oxygen  solubility  at  temperatures  higher  than  1000 ◦ C, which allows for the generation of non-stoichiometric Ta2O5-x  in addition to the generation of thermodynamic TaO2 and Ta2O5 stable phases [41].
Firstly, from 1200 ◦ C to 1600 ◦ C, the intensity of the SiO2 reflection peaks undergo a first increase and then slightly decrease process. This is mainly because as the temperature rises, the continuous passive oxidation of SiC produce a dense SiO2 layer with low oxygen diffusion coefficient [17]. However, when the temperature reaches 1500 -1600 ◦ C, a severe reaction occurs between SiC and SiO2, leading to the consumption of the SiO2 protective scale and the generation of SiO and CO [42].

question: What melting point values have been reported for ultra-high-temperature ceramics generally versus hafnium and zirconium diborides, carbides, and their oxides specifically?
answer: Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are generally defined as materials with melting points above 3000 °C [1]. Specifically, hafnium diboride (HfB2) has a melting point of approximately 3380 °C [3], while zirconium diboride (ZrB2) has a melting point of around 3200 °C [4]. Hafnium carbide (HfC) has an even higher melting point, reported to be approximately 3900 °C [2]. Additionally, the oxide of hafnium, hafnia (HfO2), has a high melting point of approximately 2758 °C [15], and the oxide of zirconium, zirconia (ZrO2), has a melting point of approximately 2500 °C [17].

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are widely used in the field of thermal protection due to their exceptional melting point ( > 30 0 0 ° C), outstanding high-temperature performance, and excellent resistance to ablation [ 1-3 ]. UHTCs are primarily composed of transition metal carbides, borides, and nitrides [ 4 , 5 ]. Among these ceramics, carbides, and nitrides can be solutionized into carbon-nitride ceramics with superior properties due to the same NaCl face-centered cubic (FCC) structure [ 6 , 7 ].
Particularly, hafnium carbide (HfC), characterized by ultra-high temperature melting point ( ∼ 3900 ° C) [ 8 , 9 ], displays enhanced performance as nitrogen atoms is incorporated [ 10 , 11 ]. In recent years, Hong et al. 1. Introduction
Hafnium diboride (HfB2) is a refractory metal boride that exhibits excellent  thermal  and  mechanical  properties,  including  high  melting point  (Tm~3380 ◦ C),  high  hardness  (~28  GPa),  and  good  oxidation resistance  [1 -4].  These  properties  make  it  a  promising  material  for extreme environments, such as protective tiles on nose cones and leading  edges  of  re-entry  vehicles.  Despite  the  promising  combination  of properties,  monolith  HfB2  has  limited  applications  owing  to  its  poor sinterability.  The  high  melting  point  and  strong  covalent  bonding  of HfB2 pose  challenges  to  its  sintering  process,  impeding  complete densification  and  leading  to  significant  grain  growth  during  conventional sintering (T > 0.5Tm) [3].
To address these challenges, various sintering techniques, such as hot pressing  (HP)  [1,5 -7],  pressure-less  sintering  (PS)  [8],  reactive  hot pressing (RHP) [9], self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10], and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [11 -18], have been employed to enhance  the  densification  of  HfB2-based  composites.  Overall,  these studies demonstrate that SPS is an effective technique for consolidating HfB2 ceramics with high relative density (up to ~ 98 %) and homogeneous  microstructure  [3,4,15 -20]. 1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC) possessing high thermomechanical strength and chemical resistance. With a melting point of ∼ 3200 ◦ C it is one of the leading candidates for thermal protection systems in hypersonic flight and refractory materials such as crucibles for high temperature metals processing [1,2]. However, its densification is challenging even by the use of pressure and pulsed electric current/spark plasma sintering (SPS) methods.
Densification of ZrB2 powders can be improved by additives. However, the selection of the additive for refractory applications can be crucial to thermomechanical strength. Higher melting point metals such as Ir, W, Nb, and Mo are often selected in up to

3.1. Melting of Hf(C,N)
In [10], an experiment was performed with simultaneous heating by direct current in graphite cells of HfC  and  Hf(C,N)  samples,  which,  according  to visual and microstructural analyses, showed melting of HfC and no traces of melting in Hf(C,N). From this  experiment,  it  was  concluded  that Tm Hf(C,N) exceeds the melting point of HfC. However, according  to  the  data  obtained  in  this  article,  the  largest T sol ≈ 3830 K (for ε λ n = 0.6) of carbonitride Hf(C,N) is below T sol ≈ 4000 K of hafnium carbide [5]; i.e., the carbonitride  should  have  started  to  melt  before  the carbide. The resulting contradiction can be resolved if we assume (another interpretation of experiment [10]) that in experiment [10], contact melting of the HfC sample occurred, which began in the contact zone of HfC  with  carbon  at  the  eutectic  temperature  of HfC +  C,  which  is  below T sol of  hafnium  carbide. Indeed, in [4, 9, 18], the temperatures of the eutectic HfC x + C T eut   = 3446-3466 K are given, which are lower  that T sol of  HfC  and T sol ≈  3700  ±  140  K  for Hf(C,N) in the present work and are obviously lower than the possible eutectic of Hf(C,N) + C (if any). It should be noted that at such a high temperature and small sample thickness (~1-2 mm), contact melting occurred quite quickly, which was recorded in [10].

3.4. Multiphase UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
For example, the melting point of metallic Zr is 1855 ◦ C, while Zr-Si can form two eutectic alloys with lower melting points-Zr9Si1 (melting point: 1570 ◦ C) and Zr1Si9 (melting point: 1370 ◦ C)-allowing for RMI processes at lower temperatures; (2) the thermal expansion coefficients of SiC are close to those of C/C, significantly lower than those of UHTCs and their oxides, preventing micro-cracks or damage to carbon fibers during composite preparation; (3) during ablation, SiO2 exhibits excellent fluidity, which helps compensate for defects caused by oxidation of UHTCs, addressing issues of oxide cracking and delamination from the matrix; and (4) the formation of SiO2-UHTCs oxide symbiotic structures or silicates further resists the erosion of flame jets and oxygen penetration, thus better protecting the C/C matrix [71,121].

KEYWORDS
Developing novel ultra-high-melting-point UHTCs with enhanced elevated-temperature mechanical performance and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance is imperative to address the critical thermal barrier in hypersonic vehicle TPS. 7,8 Hafnium carbonitride (HfCN) ceramics, with a melting point of up to 4419 ± 37 K, have become the material with the highest melting point among all discovered compounds, and have gradually received high attention in TPS in recent years. 9-11 Suvorova et al. 11 synthesized HfCN ceramics via spark plasma sintering (SPS), followed by evaluation of their mechanical properties and oxidation resistance at 1200 ◦ C. Zeng et al. 12 synthesized HfCN ceramic powder via hafnium nitride carbonization and consolidated it by SPS, demonstrating superior ablation resistance over commercial HfC. Meanwhile, Peng et al. 13 investigated the ablation/oxidation behavior of HfCN ceramics under ultra-high temperatures (3273 K), revealing a 90% reduction in linear ablation rate compared to monolithic HfC ceramics. In addition, Xu et al. 14 fabricated HfCN via hot-pressing sintering, assessing its thermomechanical properties from RT-2000 ◦ C under Ar and revealing significant C/N ratio dependence of mechanical properties in HfC x N 1-x ceramics. The existing studies have preliminarily shown that HfCN ceramics possess significant potential for application in TPS of hypersonic vehicles. However, most high-temperature mechanical properties research on HfCN materials has focused on argon or vacuum environments, and the hightemperature mechanical properties of HfCN ceramics in air environments lack systematic investigation, which is critical to the safety and application in TPS. Furthermore, current fabrication restricts bulk HfCN ceramics to diameters below 50 mm. Addressing these gaps requires, there is a pressing demand for the development of large-scale HfCN ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
However, silicon based PDCs typically undergo oxidation at these ultra-high temperatures to release SiO(g) which can lead to erosion and catastrophic failure of the material. In this regard, diborides and carbides of the early transition metals (Group III to Group VII) exhibit high melting temperatures, high strength and thermal conductivity coupled with excellent chemical stability making them ideal candidates for a variety  of  thermo-structural  applications.  Zirconium  based  diborides and carbides are particularly attractive with their high melting points ( > 3200 ◦ C), but the powder processing route for these materials limits processability; and sinterability remains a challenge [2,24,25]. In order to overcome these drawbacks, synthesis of precursors that can eventually  be  transformed  to  ceramics  containing  ultra-high  temperature phases such as borides and carbides of zirconium is of interest. It is imperative that rare earth additions such as La can be used to achieve enhanced oxidation resistance for ZrB2  and ZrC by the formation of a protective layer of La2Zr2O7  which effectively limits the inward diffusion of oxygen [26,27].

1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are those with extremely high melting points that give them a potential advantage for use in thermal protection systems or refractory applications. In particular, zirconium and hafnium diborides and carbides have melting points in excess of 3000 ◦ C. 1 In addition, diborides have higher thermal conductivity than carbides which make them promising candidates in thermal protection systems for hypersonic applications. The combination of high temperature capability and high thermal conductivity is particularly beneficial. However, ZrB2 and HfB2 baseline UHTCs possess insufficient oxidation resistance for hypersonic flight environments, in which temperatures above 2000 ◦ C in highly oxidizing atmospheres are reached. Most research to date has focused on developing more oxidation-resistant UHTCs composites. 2-5 The best oxidation resistance has been found for UHTCs containing SiC, typically additions between 10 and

Generally,  Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  refer  to  ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ C, including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Among them, HfC and TaC are extensively studied [5]. Due  to  the  high melting  point  and  superior  thermophysical  and  oxidation-resistant properties inherited from TaC and HfC, Ta4HfC5  holds promising applications in extreme high-temperature aerospace environments [8 -10].
To apply Ta4HfC5 in thermal protection materials, two fundamental

INTRODUCTION
With laser pulse heating (heating time about 1 s), the  authors  of  [4]  found  that  the  congruent  melting temperature of TaC Tm = 4277 ± 12 K is higher than the melting point of HfC Tm =  4250  ±  20  K.  From other studies [5-7] with rapid microsecond heating, it follows  that  the  most  refractory  is  mixed  carbide Ta0.8 Hf0.2 C with a melting point 4300 ± 80 K [7]. For HfC and TaC in [5, 6], the literature data on the radiative characteristics at temperatures below the melting point  were  used.  A  consequence  of  this  was  an increased  temperature  measurement  error,  approximately equal to the difference in the melting temperatures of the mentioned carbides.
In theoretical study [8], the question of substances with maximum melting temperatures was considered and it was predicted that the maximum melting temperature, exceeding all known ones, will be that of hafnium carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.38 . Review [9] provides a detailed analysis and comparison of the properties of HfC, HfN, and Hf(C,N); in accordance with [8], a conclusion is drawn that the carbonitride HfC 0.51 N0.38 may have a record high melting point 4321-4445 K. In stationary experiment [10], HfC carbide and hafnium  carbonitride  HfC 0.5 N0.35 were  heated  under identical  conditions,  which  yielded  the  melting  of HfC,  while  hafnium  carbonitride  remained  in  an unmelted state.
In this article, the physical properties of carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.32 are studied for the first time based on a fast pulse experiment (below, this carbonitride is denoted Hf(C,N)) at high temperatures in solid and liquid  states,  including  the  melting  region,  and  its melting point is estimated.

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is gaining substantial interest due to its high melting point (3245 °C), good oxidation resistance from room temperature to 2500 °C, low theoretical density, high thermal conductivity and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) exhibits the intriguing combination of both metal-like and ceramic-like properties such as high melting point (3245 °C), low coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion, high thermal conductivity, low theoretical density and ability to retain strength at elevated temperature [1]. These properties enable ZrB2 to survive extreme temperatures, chemical attack, erosive environments and heat /uniFB02 uxes, which makes it a potential candidate for hypersonic vehicles and Reusable Launch Vehicles (RLVs). ZrB2 exhibit hexagonal close pack arrangement with a strong covalent bond between B -B atoms and Zr -B atoms and the close-packed metallic layer (Zr -Zr) exhibit metallic bond characteristics like high thermal conductivity, high electrical conductivity [2]. The properties of the ZrB2 ceramic generally depend on the pyrolysis temperature and the method employed for its synthesis. Most common methods employed for the synthesis of ZrB2 is by using zirconium oxide (ZrO2), diboron trioxide (B2O3) and carbon (carbothermal reduction), ZrO2 and boron carbide (B4C) (boron carbide reduction) and ZrO2, B4C and carbon (combined reduction) [3,4] and the reactions involved are given in equations (1) -(3). Synthesis of ZrB2 with ZrO2 as the starting material is studied extensively due to low cost and easy availability of zirconium oxide, but pure ZrB2 powders are

A B S T R A C T

2.6 Binary Carbides and Borides
The first report that solid solutions of two simple ultrarefractory carbides can have an exceptionally high melting point appeared back in the 1930s 142 and this idea was periodically confirmed through both theoretical and experimental studies. 143-145 These systems are considered highly promising for combustion synthesis applications. Each of the simple carbides can be synthesized from elements via combustion mode; therefore, a mixture of three elemental powders-two metals and carbon in appropriate proportions-can also react in this manner.
However, the key question concerns the phase composition of the combustion product: will it be a mixture of two simple carbides, or will it form a single-phase binary carbide (solid solution)? To illustrate, consider the wellknown binary carbide Ta 4 HfC 5 , which can be regarded as a solid solution of two simple carbides: 4TaC + HfC. Each of these carbides can be obtained through combustion synthesis via the reactions Ta + C → TaC (∆H =-145 kJ/mol) and Hf + C → HfC (∆H =-227 kJ/mol).
A self-sustaining exothermic reaction involving a mixture of 4Ta + Hf + 5C may yield either a single-phase Ta4 HfC 5 or a mixture of Ta rich (Hf,Ta)C and Hf rich (Hf,Ta)C phases, depending on the specific conditions of the combustion synthesis. Since hafnium (melting point approximately 2233 ◦ C) has a much lower melting point than tantalum (approximately 3017 ◦ C), it is expected that hafnium reacts with graphite first to form HfC grains before tantalum reacts with carbon. Under such circumstances, the short duration of the combustion wave may not allow complete dissolution of the simple carbides HfC and TaC into each other.

The melt and PyC are separated by the reaction-formed ZrC layer (Figure 10(c)). Carbon atoms diffuse through the ZrC layer and arrive at the interface between ZrC and the melt, and the subsequent reaction continues. With the proceeding of the reaction between zirconium and carbon, the concentration of zirconium in the melt decreases and Zr 3 Si phase derived from the reaction of silicon with zirconium begins to precipitate form the melt(Figure 10(c)), which is indicated by the phase diagram of Zr-Si system (Figure 11). Zr 3 Si phase, with the melting point of 1650 ° C, is in the liquid condition at the RMI temperature. Atoms in the melt diffuse rapidly and the reaction between zirconium and carbon still progresses at a rather high speed. With the further decrease of the zirconium content in the melt, all the eutectic Zr-Si melt changes to Zr 3 Si melt and then the Zr 3 Si melt totally transfers to Zr 2 Si phase duo to the  significant  decrease  of  zirconium  content  (Figure  10  (d-e)).  Zr 2 Si,  with  the melting point of 1925 ° C, is in solid state during the RMI process. Atoms in the solid Zr 2 Si diffuse slowly and the formation of ZrC goes along at a rather low speed. Consequently, ZrC and Zr 2 Si were formed in the as-received C/ZrC composite after RMI process (1800 ° C, 30min). ZrC distributes around the PyC and Zr 2 Si locates in the middle of pores surrounded by ZrC (Figure 10(f)).

The ablation process of the Ta4HfC5 phase is summarized in Fig. 12. Among the products, HfO2  has a high melting point of approximately 2758 ◦ C [37], making it the oxide with the highest melting point except for  radioactive  element  oxides  such  as  ThO2.  Moreover,  HfO2  has  a relatively low vapor pressure, which can be only 3 MPa at a high temperature  of  2667 ◦ C  and  is  the  lowest  among  known  oxides.  Consequently,  HfO2 exhibits  remarkable  structural  stability  at  elevated temperatures, allowing it to function effectively as a skeletal support in oxidation products. However, due to the low total content, the role of HfO2  skeleton is not significant and was not observed in the ablated images shown in Fig. 9. Ta2O5, with a lower melting point of about 1850 ◦ C [38], formed a molten state in the ablation environment where temperatures exceed its melting point in Region I, and mixed with HfO2, with slightly higher content on the surface. Generally, in the central ablation pit Region I and oxidation Region II, the content of Ta2O5 in the sheet-like oxidation products is much higher than that of HfO2. However, there may also be aggregation of HfO2  content in small dimensional regions (as indicated by point 3 in Fig. 9).

1. Introduction
Di-borides, carbides, and nitrides belong to a class of materials with melting points > 2500 °C, and are therefore referred to as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Recently, zirconium di-boride (ZrB2) based UHTCs have gained popularity among the researchers, because of their unique properties like high melting temperature, high hardness, high thermal and electrical conductivity, moderate theoretical density, lower coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion as well as higher physical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1 -4]. Such exclusive properties are very much needed for critical applications like thermal protection systems of re-entry vehicles, wing leading edges of hypersonic vehicles, rocket nozzles, and plasma electrodes [2,5,6]. It is well known that reinforcing ZrB2 with SiC particles increases its /uniFB02 exural strength, fracture toughness, thermal conductivity, and oxidation resistance by a signi /uniFB01 cant amount [4,7 -10]. Recent studies have shown the optimum SiC content to be ~15 -20 vol% for achieving a desirable combination of thermal, oxidation, and ablation properties [8]. In spite of the several known positive attributes of the ZrB2 -SiC composite, its widespread application is limited by poor sinterability due to covalent bonding, high melting point, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coe /uniFB03 cient of both ZrB2 and

1. Introduction
Certain characteristics of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials containing transition metal borides and carbides give them a potential advantage over more traditional materials for use in hypersonic applications. In particular, hafnium and zirconium diborides and carbides, as well as their oxides, hafnia and zirconia, have extremely high melting points, all in excess of 2500 ◦ C (4530 ◦ F). 1 While HfC and ZrC have higher melting points than HfB2 and ZrB2, the diborides have substantially higher thermal conductivities than the carbides. 2,3 This combination of high-temperature capability and high thermal conductivity makes HfB2 and ZrB2 particularly attractive for use in sharp wing leading edges (WLEs) and nose tips. 4 In practice, the high-temperature oxidation resistance of pure diboride materials is not sufficient for aerothermal flight environments. The best oxidation performance is found for monolithic materials hot-pressed from mixtures of ceramic powders containing a silica former as a minor component. 5,6 The most common compositions typically contain 10-30 vol.% SiC, 7-12 although other silica formers like MoSi2 and TaSi2 have also been investigated. 13-16
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 650 859 2667; fax: +1 650 859 6196. E-mail address: jochen.marschall@sri.com (J. Marschall).
The design of high-performance hypersonic vehicles generally involves relatively sharp nose tips and WLEs. (An arcjet model of a concept UHTC WLE section is shown in Fig. 1.) While not an achievable design in practice, in the context of supersonic flow theory, a 'sharp' leading edge is one with zero radius of curvature. In a practical design context, 'sharp' usually refers to a leading-edge radius that is much smaller than the nose, wing, or vehicle length scale, while 'blunt' implies the converse.

Fig. 3 compares experimental melting points data and predicted melting points data. Obviously, the BPNN predicted the melting temperature of di /uniFB00 erent UHTCs systems precisely. The average absolute relative error (AARE) of the predicted results and the experimental results is as low as 5.09%, and R is 0.935. It can be seen directly in Fig. 3 that most of the data points are in the darker area, which means the network we built can predict melting points of UHTCs exactly.

1. Introduction
Hafnium  diboride  (HfB2)  has  a  hexagonal  crystal  structure  (MB2 type, P6/mmm space group) consisting of B atoms layers in 2D graphitic rings and hexagonal close-packed Hf layer [1], which endows HfB2 with high melting point (3250 ◦ C), excellent chemical stability, high electrical and thermal conductivity, good thermal shock resistance and so on [2 -4].  Therefore,  HfB2 can  be  used  as  one  of  the  most  promising ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  applied  in  some  extreme  working conditions, such as in hypersonic vehicles, rocket thrusters, and other thermal protection components [5 -9]. However, strong covalent bond and low self-diffusion coefficient of HfB2 make the densifying HfB2 very difficult [10]. In addition, the oxides (such as B2O3, HfO2) on the surface of HfB2  powder further hinder its densification sintering [11 -13]. As a consequence, some additives such as SiC [14,15], B4C [16,17], WC [18, 19], MoSi2 [20 -22], etc.) through reacting with HfO2 to reduce the onset temperature of densification are employed to densify the HfB2 powder by traditional hot pressing [23]. However, the introduction of impurities from the surplus additives and the reaction product is bound to affect the property  of  the  material.  Besides,  in  order  to  achieve  the  densifying HfB2, the long dwell time (1 -2h) at high temperature (above 2100 ◦ C) inevitably results in the grain coarsening from initial powder grain size

1 INTRODUCTION
Much research on zirconium diboride (ZrB2 )-based ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) has been carried out in the recent past. Its notable features include a high melting point ( ∼ 3246 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity ( ∼ 85-90 W/m/K 1 ), and strength retention at very high temperature ( ∼ 600 MPa at 1600 ◦ C 2 and 200 MPa at 2300 ◦ C 3 ). Over other engineering ceramics such as SiC and Si 3 N 4 , zirconium diboride has the additional advantage of high melting point of its oxide product ( ∼ 2500 ◦ C). However, the main limitation of ZrB2 -based UHTCs is related to its low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance. 4 Therefore, toughening of ceramics is the prime goal for the use of these ceramics. The reinforcing allows for ceramic toughening. Particulate, whiskers, short fibers, and continuous fibers can all be used as reinforcements. When compared to monolithic ceramics, incorporating particulate, whiskers, and short fibers improves toughening. 5 Continuous fiber, on the other hand, considerably improves the fracture toughness

question: What volume-fraction ranges have been reported for sintering additives and silica-forming secondary phases in boride-based UHTC composites?
answer:
The volume-fraction ranges for sintering additives in boride-based UHTC composites have been reported as follows:

- For ZrSi2 as a sintering additive in ZrB2 composites, a volume fraction of 30 vol% has been used [1].
- For MoSi2 as a sintering additive in UHTC composites, a volume fraction of 15 vol% has been used [3].
- For SiC as a sintering additive in ZrB2 composites, volume fractions ranging from 10 to 20 vol% have been used [3].
- For MeSi2 (such as MoSi2, HfSi2, or WSi2) as a sintering additive in ZrB2 composites, a volume fraction of 5 vol% has been used [4].

The sources do not contain information on the volume-fraction ranges for silica-forming secondary phases.

4.1. Feasibility of EBS for UHTCs
Metal Boride UHTCs are unusual materials that possess ceramic-like properties such as high melting point, hardness, as well as metal-like properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity. The UHTC material chosen for this research is ZrB2, which has an electrical and thermal conductivity of 1.08 × 10 7 S/m and 60 W/mK, respectively, and a melting temperature of 3245 ◦ C. With ZrSi2  as a sintering additive (melting point: 1625 ◦ C), it can be selectively melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the ZrB2  powder particles together, resulting in processing temperature around 1700 ◦ C that is easily attainable by the EBM machines.  Additionally,  ZrSi2  softens  around  800 ◦ C,  hence  a  liquid phase sintering process would most likely occur at temperatures less than 1700 ◦ C by an intergranular viscous ZrSi2 phase [11].
In the current study, a representative ZrB2 UHTC was mixed with 30 vol% of ZrSi2, which acts as a densification aid. It was determined by S. Guo  et  al.  [10]  that  in  the  traditional  UHTC  sintering  process,  the addition of ZrSi2  in the 30% volume fraction is critical in maintaining good  electrical  (11.9 × 10 6 S/m)  and  thermal  conductivities  (86.81 W/mK)  without  compromising  the  mechanical  behavior.  The  high electrical conductivity of the UHTC materials makes them compatible with the EBM process. The earlier work in our group [2] was the first of its  kind  to  demonstrate  the  feasibility  of  EBM  to  process  the  UHTC mixture  and  pioneer  EBS.  Although  only  1D  melt  tracks  have  been performed, interesting consolidation mechanisms have been reported. The current study will contribute more to the findings through experimentation and subsequent characterization of single-layer builds of the ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2 powder feedstock.

Effect of Transition Metal Silicides
The extremely high melting point and the low self-diffusion coefficient make these ceramics very difficult to sinter to full density: temperatures above 2000°C and the application of pressure are necessary conditions. However these processing parameters lead to coarse microstructures, with mean grain size of the order of 20 μm and trapped porosity, all features which prevent the achievement of the full potential of the thermo-mechanical properties of UHTCs.
Several  activities  have  been  performed  in  order  to  decrease  the  severity  of  the processing conditions of UHTCs introducing sintering additives, such as metals, nitrides, carbides or silicides. In general the addition of such secondary phases does decrease the sintering temperature, but some additives have some drawbacks, especially during use at high temperature, owing to their softening and the following loss of integrity of the material.
In this chapter, composites based on borides and carbides of Zr, Hf and Ta were produced with addition of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 . These silicides were selected as sintering aids owing to their high melting point (>2100°C), their ductility above 1000°C and their capability to increase the oxidation resistance.
The microstructure of fully dense hot pressed UHTCs containing 15 vol% of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 ,  was  characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning, and transmission electron microscopy.
Based on microstructural features detected by TEM, thermodynamical calculations, and the available phase diagrams, a densification mechanism for these composites is proposed.
The mechanical properties, namely hardness, fracture toughness, Young's modulus and flexural strength at room and high temperature, were measured and compared to the properties of other ultra-high temperature ceramics produced with other sintering additives. Further, the microstructural findings were used to furnish possible explanations for the excellent high temperature performances of these composites.

A B S T R A C T
strength and good thermal conductivity, the most studied UHTCs are the ZrB2-based composites [17 -19]. In order to improve the thermo-mechanical properties, oxidation resistance and final density, borides are usually combined with silicon-containing additives in small amount,  such  as  10 -20  vol%  of  SiC  [20,21].  In  fact,  the  oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor already at 1000 ◦ C, because of the formation of a porous and non-protective ZrO2 film, as well as the evaporation  of  B2O3.  The  introduction of  SiC  promotes  the  formation  of  a surficial glassy silica phase, which protects the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C [22]. Other ceramic additives, such as Y2O3  or MoSi2 improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC composites by forming a liquid phase with the oxide impurities present on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC grains [23,24]. The presence of Y2O3 stabilizes the ZrO2 formed during oxidation in the tetragonal structure, leading to the formation of a more compact film that improves the oxidation resistance [25]. The presence of MoSi2 improves the oxidation resistance thanks to the formation of a SiO2 layer. In more detail, oxidation produces a SiO2-rich glass layer, a subsurface oxide layer, and a MoB, ZrO2, and SiO2 mixture layer [26].
In this work, the possibility of producing graded composites with a fibre gradient ranging from 0 to 50 vol% via hot pressing sintering was investigated. Three UHTC matrices were mixed with carbon fibres:
- ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC - 5 vol% Y2O3 where SiC is the oxidation resistant phase and Y2O3 is the sintering aid [4,27].
- ZrB2 -15 vol% MoSi2 where MoSi2 is both sintering and oxidation resistant aid [28,29].

A B S T R A C T
In this work, ZrB2 composites were produced containing high volumes, 40 vol%, of short carbon fiber plus 10 vol% of oxidation retardant phases. The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. The selection of these silicides was based on their compatibility with high-temperature environments, i.e. they offer melting points above 1600 °C, high stiffness and, owing to their beneficial effects on densification, high temperature strength and potential oxidation resistance improvement for borides [23,25-28]. Additionally, a comparative material without MeSi2 but containing the same total amount of SH additives, i.e. 10 vol% SiC, was also prepared as a reference baseline. The question posed by this work was whether the replacement of 5 vol% of SiC with 5 vol% of MeSi2 would significantly improve the ablation resistance. Thus the UHTCMCs were exposed to the flame of an oxyacetylene torch (OAT) at temperatures above 2000 °C for 60 s. The analysis of the as-sintered and oxidized materials is presented and discussed, with the focus being on the effect of the secondary phases in promoting the formation of an ablation resistant microstructure.
The novelty of this work lies in the production and successful testing of a new class of UHTCMCs that can survive extreme conditions of oxidation at up to 2000 °C.

(B) are stacked in alternate sequence (ZrBZrBZrB). The strong covalent bonding between B-B and Zr-B atoms results in high melting temperature, hardness and strength. The high electrical and thermal conductivity are due to the close packed Zr layers that show a metallic-like bonding.
Densification of UHTCs is extremely difficult due to its high melting point, strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion rates (Sonber & Suri, 2011). In the case of borides, oxide layers of B 2 O3 and Zr (Hf-) O 2 covering the surface of boride  particles  are  subjected  to  evaporation-condensation  mechanisms  at  low temperature resulting in mass transfer without densification. At higher temperature grain coarsening becomes predominant over densification and finally causes poor mechanical properties. Temperature above 1800°C and high external pressure are needed to achieve densification in monolithic borides thanks to the activation of grain boundary and volume diffusion mechanisms. Alternatively, densification of borides can be performed at lower temperatures and pressure through either addition of sintering agents or decrease of powder mean grain size and/or increase of the defect concentration through mechanical activation (high energy milling).
Sintering additives may be ceramics or metals. They can either form a liquid phase at the sintering temperature (liquid phase sintering) or may react with and remove oxidized surface film from boride powder particles inducing a solid state sintering densification.

[30], it has been reported that the ZrB2 -SiC composites with higher reinforcement content depict greater densi /uniFB01 cation indicating that besides the sintering additives, the SiC particles could have a role as well which needs to be fully understood. The effect of SiC volume fraction on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior needs to be examined synergistically with emphasis on understanding of the operative mechanisms of additives. Moreover, there is need to arrive at optimum temperature and duration of sintering, so as to obtain desired combination of relative density, microstructure and mechanical properties. Therefore, the present report is focused on studies comprising pressureless sintering of the ZrB2 -(2 -10 vol%) SiC composites at 2000 1 C for 2 h in order to examine the effects of carbon additive, as well as SiC content on their densi /uniFB01 cation, matrix grain size, and mechanical properties.

1. Introduction
All the so-called ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs; i.e., borides, carbides, and nitrides of the early-transition metals [1]) have poor intrinsic sinterability due to the serious kinetic restrictions imposed by their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients. This is further aggravated extrinsically because the starting powders normally comprise large particles of various microns in size and with surface oxide impurities. Not surprisingly, dedicated sintering studies constitute a large part of the research effort carried out on UHTCs, and particularly on ZrB2-based UHTCs [2,3]. They have enabled the identi /uniFB01 cation of appropriate sintering additives and comminution practices. Some of these suitable additives are carbon [4,5], B4C [6], WC [7,8], and B4C þ C [9,10], which favor the conventional solid-state sintering of

This has been proven both for pressureless sintering (PS) and hot-pressing (HP), both of which apply prolonged sintering cycles (i.e., various hours). Apart from facilitating the conventional sintering, cleaning oxides off of the grain boundaries also results in improved high-temperature strength [5,8]. Furthermore, solid-state sintering additives are normally used synergistically with the wet-attrition milling of the starting powders because solid-state sinterability scales inversely with a power of the particle size (i.e., Herring's scaling law [11]).

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior

The former composite yielded a fracture strength value of 909 MPa while as the later one showed 386 MPa [156]. Thus, it can be concluded that the limitation of lower sinterability in di-borides can be overcome by judicial selection of sintering route, and size and type of reinforcement.
In many applications of UHTCs, especially in the context of their use in a space vehicle, it is mandatory to observe the role of high temperature and surrounding atmosphere during re-entry. Thus, the oxidation resistance becomes a key property to be evaluated by simulating the reentry conditions, which is discussed in section 4.2.3.

5. Densification, microstructure and mechanical properties
As discussed in the previous section, various sintering techniques (both conventional and advanced) have been used to densify transition metal di-boride based materials, with the monolithic di-borides usually necessitating very high sintering temperatures. The reduction of starting powders particle size and the use of sinter-additives are possibly the major approaches that are used to address this issue (see Figs. 7 and 9). Both metallic and non-metallic sinter-additives have been used to obtain fully dense di-boride ceramics at low sintering temperatures. While selecting the sinter-additive, the type and amount must be judiciously chosen as they influence densification, microstructure, phase assemblage and properties of the di-borides. Metallic additives (generally < 2 wt% added to borides) such as Ni, Cu, Co, Cr and Fe etc. are advantageous towards obtaining near-theoretical sinter-density, reducing the sintering temperature considerably due to liquid phase sintering and very effective in improving room temperature mechanical properties of borides [5,9,10,50,204]. However, discussion on the use of metallic sinter-additives is not included here since the addition of metallic additives degrades the high temperature properties of the di-boride ceramics, eventually rendering them unsuitable for the ultra-high temperature applications. For example, the strength of 4 wt% Ni added ZrB2 increased from 370 to 630 MPa with increasing temperature (up to 800 °C) and further increasing the temperature resulted drastic reduction in strength (240 MPa at 1000 °C and 20 MPa at 1200 °C) [10]. Hence, the effects of non-metallic sinter-additives on the densification and microstructure of the important di-boride ceramics have been discussed in the following sub-sections.

ABSTRACT
A detailed review of the processing of zirconium, hafnium, and tantalum based boride-carbide-nitride composites is presented. The processing methodology and important steps involved in producing a pore-free microstructure are reported. The effect  of  addition  of  secondary  and  ternary  compounds  on  densification  is  highlighted as is the reactive processing of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on zirconium carbide through the formation of a transient non-stoichiometric carbide and transient liquid phase, which enable densification at much lower temperatures. The reactive processing method is promising in that it readily leads to variation in the composition of secondary/ternary non-oxide phases in the composites as well as the incorporation of fibres which may otherwise degrade. Since the processing temperatures are lower, the grain size obtained after densification is finer and may lead to better mechanical properties (hardness, fracture toughness, and strength). Processing of fibre based composites with boride particulates and silicon carbide through the ceramic precursor route are also discussed.

UHTC ceramics derived from B 4 C and MAX phases by reactive sintering
Dawid Kozień 1  , Adrian Graboś 2 , Katarzyna Pasiut 1 , Magdalena Ziąbka 1 , Leszek Chlubny 1 , Marcin Wójtowicz 1 , Wojciech Banaś 1 , Marek Grabowy 3  & Zbigniew Pędzich 1
In this work, the influence of three MAX phases (Ti 3 SiC 2, Ti 2 AlC, and Cr 2 AlC) on the densification and final properties of dense composites from Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) family was studied. Addition of the MAX phases resulted in the formation of secondary boride phases due to chemical reactions between boron carbide and MAX phase. Mentioned phases were utilized for reduction of sintering temperature of the final composite material. It decreased the sintering temperature up to 800 °C when compared to pure B 4 C. Additionally, the phase composition and derivative mechanical properties were investigated to evaluate differences between final composite materials. All obtained materials remarkably increased their fracture resistance (K IC ) from 33 to 100%. The mechanical properties of B 4 C were either retained (Ti 3 SiC 2 ) or decreased in terms of Vickers hardness and Young's Modulus (Ti 2 AlC and Cr 2 AlC). Systems with Ti 3 SiC 2 appeared to possess significant potential for application, also when compared to UHTC systems of similar purpose.
Keywords Boron carbide, Ceramic matrix composites, MAX phases, Ultra-high temperature ceramics

With more testing of Zr/HfB2-SiC ceramics in environments more closely emulating atmospheric re-entry and hypersonic cruise applications,  strategies  moved  away  from  silica-based  scales.  Sintering/
