2. Experimental procedure
Large agglomerates of SiC arise from the density difference between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and HfB2 ( ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 ), which promotes their non-homogeneous mixing during thermo-rotary drying of HfB2-based UHTCs solutions. The use of a surfactant might overcome this limitation and is being investigated. HS20La contains some SiC agglomerates of ∼ 10 𝛍 m, which is about half the size of the HS20 agglomerates. The location of La2O3 ( ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 ) particles near to SiC particles and its closer density to SiC than HfB2 assist particle dispersion in the solutions reducing the formation of SiC agglomerates. However, SiC distribution is more homogeneous throughout ZrB2 matrix than HfB2 matrix due to lower density difference between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and ZrB2 ( ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 ) than between SiC ( ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 ) and HfB2 ( ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 ), which prevents second phase particles agglomeration in UHTCs solutions.

3. Results
All samples attained high density values, higher than 99% of theoretical density (TD) except ZrB2 which was 95.8%. Theoretical density was calculated using the rule of mixtures. Table 2 shows SPS conditions and density values measured after sintering. Phase analysis by XRD confirmed all starting phases remained in UHTCs after SPS.
Fig. 1 shows secondary electron (SE) images of ground, polished and etched UHTCs surfaces after SPS. ZrB2 are the larger grey grains (2-6 𝛍 m), finer dark grains are SiC (1-2 𝛍 m) and light grey grains are La2O3 ( ∼ 1 𝛍 m) difficult to distinguish from ZrB2 grains, which are, respectively, labelled with 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 1c as they were confirmed with EDS. Homogeneous dispersion of SiC in ZS20 and SiC/La2O3 in ZS20La is found in the ZrB2 matrix. SiC and La2O3 are usually located together forming small agglomerates as an interconnected network in the ZrB2 and HfB2 matrix. However, less than 5% of SiC and La2O3 particles were detected separately. On the other hand, SiC agglomerates as large as 20-30 𝛍 m were found in HfB2-based composites as seen in Fig. 1b indicated by an arrow.

3.3.2. ZS20La
ZS20La showed pure parabolic behavior at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2) and its oxidation kinetic exponent increases with temperature to
∼ 2.5 at 1500 ◦ C. In addition, values of n for mass gain and oxide scale thickness at 1600 ◦ C are different by almost four times, 2.1 for mass gain and 7.5 for oxide scale, which indicates that an oxygen adsorption mechanism may be active because large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20La is smaller than oxide scale thickness of ZS20 at 1400 ◦ C while the converse applies for 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C as seen in Fig. 2, presumably due to the higher volatilization of B2O3 present in the BS layer of ZS20 at these temperatures. Therefore, ZS20La is more oxidation resistant at 1400 ◦ C but not at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. The main difference between the oxide layers of ZS20 and ZS20La is the mixing of the BS glassy melt with the ZrO2 scale which is more viscous in ZS20La and does not ascend directly to the top surface, producing a more homogeneous mixing between glassy and oxide phase as seen if Fig. 7 is compared to the ZS20 oxide layer in Fig. 5a or b. Homogeneous mixing of BS melt with ZrO2 could be detrimental for the system as diffusion through ZrO2 is much quicker than through a BS melt even if it has higher viscosity due to the presence of La2O3. La2O3 proximity to SiC promotes the inclusion of La2O3 into the BS phase, increasing viscosity and thermal stability as well as lowering the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the glassy phase. In fact, La2O3 is used as an additive to increase creep resistance of MoSi2, 45 which contains glassy SiO2 at grain boundaries and triple junctions. Reaction was complete or SiO2 was completely eliminated at a La2O3/SiO2 wt.% ratio of 3.2. 45 Two different polymorphs of La2Si2O7 were observed with a wt.% ratio of 6.4, which corresponds to a 4 wt.% La2O3/MoSi2 composites ( ∼ 5 vol.%). Therefore, in the present study La2O3 is completely dissolved in the La2O3-doped UHTCs BS melt for <5 vol.% La2O3content andprotective Me2La2O7 is not formed unless La2O3 concentration is at least 10 vol.%. 28 However, precipitates of La2O3-SiO2 phase were found on top of the oxide layer and identified with EDS in samples oxidized at 1500 ◦ C as observed in Fig. 7a indicated by the arrow, which confirms higher B2O3 volatilization at 1500 ◦ C than at 1400 ◦ C, facilitating the precipitation of La2O3-SiO2 particles. Furthermore, a completely new phenomena was observed on samples oxidized at 1600 ◦ C.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited a high-temperature strength of 390 ± 21 MPa at 1800°C. This compared to the undoped version (ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2), which had a high-temperature strength of 34 ± 6 MPa at 1800°C [6][7].

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.
be prepared at a lower sintering temperature of ~1600 ◦ C [19,23 -25].

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. Meanwhile, the densification temperature of C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics.  Thus,  there  is  a

4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

For comparison, a ZrB2-based UHTC with unconverted sintering additive (ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2, labeled as ZS15) was also tested under the same conditions. 3.3. High-temperature flexural strength

From the stress-strain  curves,  the  load  of  ZC1600-2h  increased  almost  linearly until reaching a maximum value, and then sharply decreased after the final  brittle  fracture.  As  shown  in  Fig.  6(b),  it  confirmed  that  the ZC1600-2h  specimen  was  broken  into  two  pieces  without  obvious plastic deformation (Fig. 6(b)). In contrast, the ZS15 specimen yielded at only approximately 30 MPa, and then steadily deformed with a large strain at a low stress of 30 -34 MPa. The low yield strength was attributed to the presence of Zr -Si phases with a low-melting point. It has been reported that the low melting point phases would significantly reduce the  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs  [38,39].  After flexural test, the ZS15 specimen was obviously bent and not completely broken into two pieces, as displayed in Fig. 6(c). The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of  magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa), as shown in Table 2. Compared  to  its  room-temperature  strength  of  494 ± 2  MPa,  the high-temperature strength retention ratio (the ratio of 1800 ◦ C-strength to RT-strength) of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).

Notably, the use of pitch fibre in the same study has led to a flexural strength of 260 ± 50 MPa, which is somewhat comparable to the values obtained for the flexural tests in the current research. On the other hand, Zhang et al. [47] have reported a significant increase in the flexural strength of nano-slurry infiltrated and hot-pressed 2D-Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite from 196 ± 14 MPa to 234 ± 18 MPa through the heat treatment of Cf at 1800 ◦ C. [23] have obtained a flexural strength value of 199 ± 26 MPa, which is somewhat comparable to the range of σ f values obtained in the present work.

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
The flexural strength of hot pressed ZrB2 tested between room temperature and 1600 ◦ C showed a value of 360 MPa at room temperature decreasing to a minimum of 170 MPa. The same ceramic when tested in Ar  between  1500 ◦ C  and  2300 ◦ C  raised  its  strength  to  300  MPa  at 2200 ◦ C, which then lowered down to 220 MPa at 2300 ◦ C [151]. A historic study by Rhodes [143] demonstrated the increase in bending strength  of  ZrB2  from  325  MPa  at  room  temperature  to  420  MPa  at 800 ◦ C. Further, increase in temperature reduced the strength to 145 MPa at 1400 ◦ C.  Bend strength again raised to 200 MPa at 1900 ◦ C followed by a drop at 2200 ◦ C to 50 MPa. The initial rise in bend strength was ascribed to the thermal residual stresses relieving at higher temperatures. The second rise in bend strength was attributed to the plastic flow from creep, again relieving the stresses [143]. ZrB2  ceramic composite (with 5 vol % WC + 3 vol % SiC) developed by Silvestroni et al. [152] retained a flexural strength of 836 MPa at 1800 ◦ C. The elevated temperature bend strength of some ZrB2  and HfB2  based ceramics is illustrated  in  Fig.  18.  Particulate  reinforced  di-borides  especially  SiC
reinforced composites display improved fracture toughness up to ~6 MPam 1/2 , hardness ~26 GPa and flexural strength ~1000 MPa [136] (while most others have exhibited flexural strength of ~650 MPa), while retaining flexural strength at high temperatures (~850 MPa at 1500 ◦ C).

question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
answer:
In Liu et al. (2023), the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolved as follows during hot-press sintering: at 1350°C, the polished surface was quite rough with a lot of pores, and the loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr-Si-C cluster [1]. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450°C, the pores became less, and the Zr-Si-C cluster precipitated spheres with a diameter of ~85 nm [1]. After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 30 min, the Zr-Si-C cluster transformed into a core-shell phase, with the core being carbide enriched Zr and the shell being carbide enriched Si [1]. After sintering at 1600°C for 2 h, the Zr-Si-C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC [9].

The final state of the Zr-Si-C phases was that they were almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC [9].

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦ C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)). Fig.  3(b,  d,  f,  h)  shows  the  high  magnification  images  to  reveal  the morphological  evolution.  After  hot-sintering  at  1350 ◦ C,  the  loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr -Si -C cluster, as marked by the dotted ellipse in Fig. 3(b). Based on the reaction between the siliceous component and C (Reaction 4 -6), the Zr -Si -C cluster may contain ZrSi2,  ZrSi,  Si,  ZrC,  and  SiC.  It  is  difficult  to  distinguish  the various phases. With the sintering temperature increasing to 1450 ◦ C, the  Zr -Si -C cluster  precipitated  spheres  with  a  diameter  of  ~85  nm (Fig. 3(d)). After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr -Si -C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. The reactivity of Zr species with carbonaceous materials is higher than that of Si species, as can be confirmed in the erosion of carbon  fiber  by  molten  ZrSi2 [35,36],  tending  to  form  a  similar core-shell  structure  [37]. 4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Unlike the pure ZrSi2  (Fig. S1), an additional exothermic peak at 1347 ◦ C  (Fig.  1)  for  the  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  was  found  at 1200 -1400 ◦ C. Notably, the enthalpy change calculated from the peak area during 1200 -1400 ◦ C for mixed powders was 1127 J/g, which was much larger than 403 J/g of the pure ZrSi2 powders. The extra enthalpy change might be contributed by the reaction between C and ZrSi2/ZrSi/ Si, as described by the following reaction:

According to the TG-DSC results, Reactions 1 -4 may occur during heating  process.  To  further  identify  the  reactions  during  the  in-situ synthesis  path,  more  characterizations  of  XRD,  SEM,  and  EDS  were conducted in the following sections.
Fig.  2  shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  and  as-sintered  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.  As  shown  in Fig. 2, for the powders, the diffraction peaks of ZrSi2  and ZrB2  phases were clearly detected, while C was difficult to identify due to its amorphous state. After sintering at 1350 -1600 ◦ C, besides the primary ZrB2 phase,  the  diffraction  peaks  of  ZrC  and  SiC  appeared  with  the disappearance of ZrSi2  peaks, indicating the occurrence of the in-situ reactions mentioned above. With the sintering temperature increased, the intensity of ZrC and SiC diffraction peaks gradually increased, which implied the in-situ reactions were gradually deepening during 1350 -1600 ◦ C.
Fig. 2. X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders and assintered C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics.

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
In order to understand phase evolution during the in-situ reactive sintering, heat and mass changes of the mixed powders during heating process,  phase  composition  and  microstructure  of  the  as-sintered  Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics were successively investigated. Fig. 1 shows the curves of heat change vs. temperature and mass change vs. temperature for the mixed C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders. As can be seen in Fig. 1, three distinguished exothermic peaks were observed. The first exothermic peak was found at around 921 ◦ C, which may be caused by the exothermic coverage of the reaction between ZrSi2 and carbon. The second  and  third  exothermic  peaks  were  located  closely  at  around 1311 ◦ C and 1347 ◦ C, which should be related to two different reactions at 1300 -1400 ◦ C. It is well known that ZrSi2 becomes unstable with the temperature up to near its melting temperature. The formation enthalpy of ZrSi2 and ZrSi were GLYPH<0> 60 kJ/mol and GLYPH<0> 90 kJ/mol, respectively [34]. Therefore, the decomposition of ZrSi2 into ZrSi and Si was an exothermic reaction, as described by the following reaction:

trade-off between low-temperature sintering and good high-temperature strength.
In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

3.1.1. ZrC-B4C-Si (R1) series
As was shown in our previous work [16], the densification of the ZrC-B4C-Si  powder  mixture  during  hot  pressing  (Fig.  6a)  proceeds through three main stages. The first stage, providing about 30 % of the overall  sample  shrinkage, occurs  during  the sample  heating between 1000 ◦ C and 1300 ◦ C, alongside the precursor phase content decreasing by about 50 % and reaction (1) products (ZrB2 and SiC) appearing. This stage was explained by the reaction-induced powder mobility, arising from the disappearance of the initial phase grains (B4C, ZrC, and Si) alongside  the  formation  of  new  ones  (ZrB2,  SiC,  and  graphite).  The shrinkage stops at approximately 1300 ◦ C, and the sample height remains  constant  up  to  1550 ◦ C.  During  this  second  stage,  called the plateau ,  reaction  (1) is  completed  by  about  90 %,  meaning  that  the precursor phase content is now 10 % as compared to the beginning. As we can see from Fig. 6, presenting the material structure before the start of the first stage (Fig. 6b) and at the end of the second stage (Fig. 6c), the reaction (1) leads to a significant structure refinement with most of the grains after the second stage significantly less than 0.5 µ m. It should be noted that stratification was not observed either after the first or the second stage. The third stage began at 1600 ◦ C, with shrinkage occurring even faster than in the first stage, and continued until 1850 ◦ C. In [16], we suggested that the rapid consolidation at this stage originated from new phase plastic deformation and was facilitated by the rapid growth of nanostructured nuclei, the presence of molten silicon remnants, and the completion  of  reaction  (1).  This  is  the  stage  when  the  stratification occurs.

3.2. Densification behavior
ceramics during 1300 -1400 ◦ C. With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦ C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr -Si -C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure (Fig. 5 (c)), contributing to the second rapid densification stage of 1420 -1600 ◦ C (Fig. 5(f)). Due to the extrusion of the Si-rich liquid phase from the Zr -Si -C cluster, a core-shell phase with a Zr-rich interior core and a Si-rich exterior shell was formed, as shown in Figs. 5(d) and Fig. 3 (f). Then, the large core-shell phase gradually split into small ZrC/SiC particles(Fig. 5(e)). After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h, the Zr -Si -C phase was almost entirely transformed into SiC and ZrC. Correspondingly, the densification was further enhanced with the relative density of ZC16002h of 100%. In short, in this way, the sintering additive of ZrSi2 not only promoted  the  densification  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs,  but  also  was  converted into high-temperature resistant phases by C dopant, which would be benefit to lower sintering temperature and improve high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs.

2.1. Multiscale structural characteristics of UHTCs
At elevated temperatures, UHTCs undergo progressive microstructural transformations that influence mechanical performance [24,100]. Grain-boundary  segregation  of  dopants  has  been  shown  to  enhance high-temperature  strength  in  both  conventional  and  high-entropy UHTCs  (HE-UHTCs)  [24].  For  example,  Shen  et  al.  [101] demonstrated that the addition of tungsten to ZrC reduces oxide impurities, facilitates densification during sintering, and strengthens GB cohesion, thereby improving flexural strength at elevated temperatures. However, excessive tungsten content leads to the formation of brittle secondary phases and a corresponding reduction in mechanical strength, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Additionally, sintering conditions also play a critical role in regulating  the  microstructure  and  high-temperature  performance  of UHTCs. Vasylkiv et al. [102] found that the fracture behavior of spark plasma sintered TaB2  remains largely insensitive to increasing consolidation temperatures (Fig. 3(b)). Liu et al. [103] introduced active carbon into ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics, which reacted with sintering additives to form  refractory  secondary  phases.  The  resulting  C-doped  composite achieved full densification at a relatively low temperature of 1600 ◦ C and  exhibited  exceptional  flexural  strength  of  390 MPa  at  1800 ◦ C (Fig. 3(c)).

3.1. Microstructure and phase evaluation
Almost full densification for all compositions was achieved with the addition of tantalum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) to ZrB2 -SiC. Fig. 1(a) shows the  presence  of  ZrB2,  SiC,  ZrO2,  (Zr,Ta)C  and  (Zr,Ta)B2 crystalline phases  in  the  MS-SPSed  ZrB2  composites.  Fig.  1(b)  shows  the  peaks splitting in XRD from 40 ◦ angle, which indicates the presence of ZrB2 and (Zr,Ta)B2 phases. The XRD of all sintered sample in the diffraction angle of 50 -85 ◦ range characterized with the XRD peaks splitting, which indicates the change in lattice parameters of core and shell structures, which is observed in Fig.1(c and d). Fig. 1(c) and (d) shows the low and high magnification images of ZS-10Ta composite and Fig. 1(e) shows the representative EDS elemental mapping of phases formed in the sintered composite. The microstructure of ZS-10Ta consists the major phases of ZrB2  (core), (Zr,Ta)B2  (rim), SiC and minor (Zr, Ta)C phases. The EDS elemental mapping of the phases further confirmed the phases as ZrB2 (core), (Zr,Ta)B2  (rim), SiC and (Zr,Ta)C (Fig. 1(e)). It has to be noted the ZrO2 phase could not be resolved in the SEM micrographs, however, its  presence is obvious in the XRD patterns. A careful observation of microstructure  reveals  that  the  SiC  dark  contrast  phase  uniformly dispersed  throughout  the  matrix  and  it  is  present  along  the

3.1. Microstructural evolution
To explore the effect of temperature on the microstructural evolution during sintering, the cross-sectional surfaces of the pure polycrystalline ZrC samples obtained by sintering at 5.0 GPa and 800 -1500 ◦ C were characterized  by  SEM  (Fig.  2),  and  it  can  be  clearly  found  that  the microstructure of the ZrC ceramics sintered under 5.0 GPa pressure has a strong temperature dependence. At 800 ◦ C (Fig. 2(a)), compared to higher sintering temperatures, the grain boundaries are sharper. A large number of voids can be observed (Fig. 2(g)), which is due to the fact that the lower temperature does not allow the ZrC grains to yield. There is mostly mechanical bonding between the grains caused by pressure, and the bonding of the grain boundaries is not sufficiently tight. As a result, the fracture is mainly intergranular. Below 1400 ℃ (Fig. 2(b)-(d)), the pores between the grains are gradually reduced, the grain boundaries become stronger and begin to blur, and the fracture gradually changes from intergranular to transgranular. Especially at 1300 ◦ C, the porosity decreases  significantly  and  the  densification  reaches  its  maximum (Fig. 2(h)). When the sintering temperature reaches 1400 ◦ C (Fig. 2(e)), the grains start to grow abnormally, and the grain boundaries move rapidly towards the pores due to the excess free energy resulting in many pores inside the grains. With further increase in temperature to 1500 ℃ (Fig. 2(f)), the grains grow further and the grain boundaries become indistinct  and  challenging  to  identify.  There  is  a  strong  competitive relationship between grain fragmentation and growth throughout the sintering  temperature  range.  To  more  clearly  reflect  the  impact

3.2. Microstructure characterization and phase evolution of SiZrBC ceramics
Fig. 4. XRD patterns of (a) SiZrBC (BZ6) ceramics after pyrolysis at 1000 -1500 /C14 C; (b) B: Zr SiZrBC ceramics after pyrolysis at 1500 /C14 C.
The molar ratio of boron to zirconium also exerted a signi /uniFB01 cant effect on the phase of ceramics at 1500 /C14 C. Fig. 4(b) displays the XRD curves of SiZrBC ceramics. Unlike the situation with the precursor, the diffraction peaks in these XRD curves were all distinct and strong, indicating the high crystallinity of the ceramic sample. The peaks at 2 θ ¼ 33.0 /C14 (111), 38.3 /C14 (200), 55.3 /C14 (220), 65.9 /C14 (311), 69.3 /C14 (222), and 82.0 /C14 (400) were attributed to ZrC (PDF#29 -1129). In BZ1 ceramic sample, the phase composition was basically presented by SiC and ZrC, while ZrB2 was not detected. With the continuous feed of the boron elements, the intensity of the ZrB2 -related peak increased dramatically. It can be seen while the Zr -C bonds connect easier than Zr -B bonds, more Zr -B bonds form with increasing boron content. In BZ6 ceramic sample, the main phases were SiC and ZrB2 phase was absent. By using Scherrer's equation (D ¼ k γ / Bcos θ ), the average grain sizes of SiC and ZrB2 were 30 nm and 50 nm, respectively. These results indicated that the phase composition of ceramic samples could be customized by adjusting the molar ratio of boron to zirconium during the synthesis of single -source precursors and controlling the phase composition of the SiZrBC ceramics at the microstructure level.

4. Conclusion
In this study, the phase transformations, microstructural evolution, and  oxygen  diffusion  mechanisms  of  (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C -SiC  ceramics under various oxidation conditions are investigated, and their ablation mechanisms are examined. The conclusions are as follows.
- (1)  (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C-SiC ceramics show good oxidation properties, with  the  onset  oxidation  temperature  of  about  750 ◦ C.  At 1000 ◦ C, the oxide scale is primarily composed of orthorhombic (Zr, Hf, Ti)O2  and β -SiC. At around 1200 ◦ C, the main phases in

question: Huang et al. 2016 thermally cycle ZrB2-SiC and ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites over a specified hot-and-cold temperature range simulating low-Earth orbit, while Borrelli et al. 2009 assume an initial temperature at the start of the Nose_2 re-entry trajectory in their FEM model. Which is colder: the low end of Huang's thermal shock range, or Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature of 300 K?
answer: The low end of Huang's thermal shock range is -110 °C [17]. Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature is 300 K, which is equivalent to 27 °C. Therefore, the low end of Huang's thermal shock range is colder than Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature.

a b s t r a c t
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) are prepared on the surface of PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, and the thermal stability is one of the key issues for C/SiC composites. This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

Thermal shock comes from the space craft operates during shadow of the Earth and out repeatedly in low Earth orbit, the temperature range is generally between /C0 110 /C14 C and 200 /C14 C. 1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

4. Conclusions
All the UHTCMCs successfully passed the base qualification and cyclic  exposure for three times, achieving temperatures in the order of 2000 -2500 K depending on the material and corresponding emissivity. Larger amounts of SiC were conducive in maintaining the Near InfraRed (NIR) spectral emissivity around 0.6 -0.8, owing to continuous recall of silica-glass to the surface, which all contributed not to exceed 2050 K during the qualification test. On the other hand, when only 10 vol% SiC was  present  in  the  matrix,  the  NIR  emissivity  waved  in  the  0.8 -0.4 range, owing to a prevalence of zirconium oxide on the surface. For these set of composites, regardless the presence of short or long fibre, the temperature  approached  2500 K.  The  reference  Cf/SiC  loaded  with 10 vol%  ZrB2 composite,  featured  by  mostly  silica,  had  a  spectral emissivity as high as 0.8 -0.9 with stable peak temperature of 2100 K. Although such discrepancies in the experienced thermal histories, the new  UHTCMCs  outperformed  the  reference  composite  by  enhanced ablation resistance.
Reusability tests, repeated three times, were first very reproducible assessing the reliability of the method, and then confirmed the trend of the baseline qualification outcomes, verifying the capability of the novel UHTCMCs to maintain their functionality and structural integrity after repeated exposure, making them appealing for future reusable TPS.
Finally, an extremely demanding test condition was selected in order to assess the materials performance under the peak pressure conditions of  an  abort  re-entry  scenario.  This  was  achieved  by  substantially increasing  the  heat  flux  delivered  to  the  surface,  up  to  8.5 MW/m 2 . Despite the considerable erosion and the very high temperature reached (over 3000 K for all UHTCMC compositions), which even led to material melting on the exposed surface, all the UHTCMCs were able to preserve a good  structural  integrity,  without  any  catastrophic  failures,  demonstrating their ability to properly protect the spacecraft even in the most challenging re-entry environment.

a b s t r a c t
Thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-10vol%SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 was investigated by means of water-quenching as well as the finite element analysis. Optimal residual strength and critical temperature difference were obtained from ZrB2-10 vol%SiC ceramic added by 10vol%ZrO2. Simulated results revealed that ZrB2-10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 provided the lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factor during thermal shock, which confirmed the experimental data. Such improvement in thermal shock resistance compared with ZrB2-10vol%SiC was primarily ascribed to the improved toughness by the addition of ZrO2. Besides, appropriate phase transformation of ZrO2 during quenching was further beneficial to resist the thermal shock.

In the case of coatings and graded materials, theoretical and simulated progress on the thermal shock, especially the thermal stress intensity factors (SIFs), was also carried out [14]. With regard to UHTCs, F. Monteverde et al. developed thermal shock of ZrB2-15vol%SiC ceramic, as well as ZrB2-15vol%SiC doped with 10vol%HfB2 [15]. And the critical temperature difference of both ceramics was further analyzed according to the residual strength after thermal shock. In addition, only a few studies on the thermal shock of UHTCs can be found [16-18].
The present paper focused on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC based UHTCs with addition of zirconia. By means of traditional water-quenching as well as the finite element method (FEM), influence of ZrO2 on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC ceramics was analyzed.

2.2. Atmospheric Re-entry
Starting at a 120 km altitude and an initial ~ Mach 25 velocity, NASA space shuttles were required to end missions by landing on a runway 91 m wide [33], - a challenge for blunt-shaped bodies. The accuracy of a body during re-entry is related to leading edge geometry. Sharp geometries have higher ballistic coefficients (BC) and lower drag forces/coefficients than blunt geometries. However, as BC increases, so too do maximum heating rates. If a material can withstand ultra-high temperatures, the BC of the vehicle can be increased via sharper, more streamlined geometries that allow several advantages, like significantly altering the altitude of maximum deceleration. The lower the altitude of deceleration, the shorter the amount of time spent in the atmosphere, or the more rapidly the vehicle arrives at the surface of the Earth. The benefits of a high BC compared to a low BC are listed in Table 2.
Opeka et al. indicated the 500 ◦ -2000 ◦ C range as being the necessary materials window for hypersonics [34]. Technically, hypersonic velocity is defined as any speed exceeding the speed of sound, but is typically more  specifically  defined  as  beginning  at  Mach  5,  or  in  the  range 1702-3403 m/s. Hypersonic velocities are less demanding the further from the Earth ' s atmosphere due to decreasing air density. The Space Shuttle re-entering the Earth ' s  atmosphere at nearly Mach 25 in low earth  orbit  is  a  substantially  different  thermal,  aerodynamic,  and oxidation scenario than Mach 25 a few kilometers above the surface.
As discussed earlier, the maximum usage temperature for any SiC based thermal protection system (TPS) is ~1727 ◦ C and for comparative purposes, SiC is viewed as protective up to1727 ◦ C. A B S T R A C T
The in-situ exothermic reactions between ZrC0.8, B4C and Si have assisted densi /uniFB01 cation and allowed to obtain fully dense ZrB2-31 wt.%SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics within 6 min at 1750 °C. The use of zirconium carbide instead of metallic zirconium in the green body obviated the possibility of in-situ SHS process and allowed to apply the pressure at low temperatures. The latter provided a /uniFB01 rst densi /uniFB01 cation stage just above 1050 °C. A slight carbon excess was created in the green body to preserve the carbon nanotubes. The developed reactive hot pressing route (1830 °C, 3 min, 30 MPa) has been successfully used to obtain ZrB2-SiC ceramics containing 8 vol.% of multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MW-CNT). The carbon nanotubes survived the thermal cycle and could be clearly observed in the sintered ceramics. The CNT addition improved the fracture toughness of the composite from 4.3 MPa m 1/2 for ZrB2-31 wt.%SiC to 6.8 MPa m 1/2 for ZrB2-29 wt.%SiC-CNT.

1. Introduction
The design of hypersonic vehicles needs leading edge components with sharp profile to improve flight performances like drag reduction, easier manoeuvrability, or larger cross-range options during atmospheric exit and re-entry [1]. At some stage of an hypersonic (re-entry) flight, the aforementioned sharp components are subject of very stressful heat fluxes in corrosive plasmas from atmosphere, and temperatures exceeding 2300 K for several minutes may become for these components a projected service condition [2]. Such extreme conditions limit the spectrum of possible candidates that nowadays are intensively searched within the class of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC). UHTC typically include non-oxides with melting/decomposition temperatures in excess of 3300 K. Diborides of the group IV transition metals like ZrB2 and HfB2 (3518 K and 3653 K of melting point, respectively) are currently the base systems taken in greater consideration to answer this technological challenge. Besides melting points greater than 3500 K [3], transition metal diborides have also thermal conductivity above 50 W/(m K) up to 1500 K [4], giving them a great potential advantage over more traditional materials to operate in extreme environments such as those experienced

Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix Ceramic
The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was performed in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15 ºC, 1200±15 ºC and 1400±15 ºC for 30 min. Compared with the original strength of 580 MPa, the strengths for the specimen after oxidation for 30 min at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 609 MPa, 656 MPa and 660 MPa, respectively, due to the sealing of the flaws on the surface of the specimen by the oxide layer. The thermal shock resistances of the specimens before and after oxidation were measured by water quenching. The measured Δ T crit values for the specimen after oxidization at 1000 ºC, 1200 ºC and 1400 ºC were 352 °C, 453 ºC and 623 ºC, respectively, which were distinctly higher than the temperature of 270 °C for the unoxidized specimen. The improvement in thermal shock resistance was attributed to the formation of the oxide layers. The formation of oxide layers resulted in the following behaviors: (I) the healing of surface flaws, (II) an increase in flexural strength, (III) the formation of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) a decrease in thermal stress and (V) the consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects improved the thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic. The results indicate the potential of this method for improving the strength and thermal shock resistance of ZrB 2 -based ceramics.

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Not only the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance, but responses of materials to the conditions of high enthalpy plasma flows that are typically encountered during atmospheric re-entry need to be evaluated. One of the possible ways to investigate such effects, in terms of materials' response to large heat fluxes as well as oxidation resistance under extreme/complex conditions, is Arc-jet testing. A schematic representation of a typical arc-jet testing set-up is presented in Figure 14a. In a recent work, Savino et al. (Savino, 2008) had performed arc-jet testing using a 80 kW plasma torch, operating under in inert gas conditions at mass flow rates up to 5 g/s, on hemispheric shaped HfB 2 - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 and HfC - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 specimens placed at a distance of 6 cm form the exit torch (see Figure 14bd).  The  temperatures of investigation were between 1950 o C and 2400 o C, which revealed that both the  materials  could  be  stable  up  to  temperatures  exceeding  2000 o C. Prior to this work, arc-jet testing were done only on ZrB 2 -  SiC and HfB 2 -  SiC composites (Gasch, 2004). Due to the issues associated with experimental determination of such high temperature properties, a lot of work has focused on theoretical simulation of the behavior via computational fluid dynamics (Borrelli, 2010), FEM based analysis (Borrelli, 2009; Stine, 1968) or various other computational tools. Such models need to simulate the demands for the application under consideration. However, despite the progress with experimental and computational facilities, still lacking  is  a  comprehensive  thermostructural  database  for  the  various  potential UHTCs.

5. Conclusions
ZrB2-SiC based ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 were prepared by hot-pressing. By means of the water-quenching, thermal shock behavior of the ceramics was investigated with temperature difference increased from 200 to 800 ◦ C. Results of residual strength and critical temperature difference revealed that ZS10Z exhibited the optimal thermal shock resistance. Simulated analysis with FEM found that ZS10Z provided lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factors during thermal shock, which was in a good agreement with the experimental data. Improved toughness was beneficial for the thermal shock resistance. In addition, phase transformation from t-ZrO2 to m-ZrO2 during thermal shock led to the tougher surface, which was also favorable for the thermal shock resistance. With further addition of ZrO2 to ZrB2-SiC, phase transformation during thermal shock will crack the surface. In conclusion, ZrB2 -10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 wasdiscovered to provide the best thermal shock resistance, which should be instructive for the design of ZrB2 -based ultra-high temperature ceramics.

THERMAL SHOCK RESISTANCE
After the recent tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Columbia, the findings of the Columbia Accident Investigation Board and the recent Space Exploration Initiative suggest that safety be made the top priority in the design of new reentry vehicles (Wang et al., 2007). During reentry from Low Earth Orbit (LEO), such as from the Space Station or parking orbits, relatively high speeds (on the order of 8 km/s) are reached (Monteverde et al., 2007). External thermal protection systems of reusable space vehicles, in the form of rigid surfaces in areas of ultra-high working temperatures (beyond 1800 °C), must be able to withstand high temperatures, high heat flux and mechanical stresses associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry in Earth's atmosphere (Tang et al., 2007). Most ceramic matrix composites, including ZrB 2 matrix ceramics, have inherently low fracture toughness and are thus susceptible to catastrophic fractures caused by thermal stresses under the above-mentioned conditions. Consequently, the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic must be estimated.
In  the  present  work,  the  thermal  shock  resistance  of  the  ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was investigated by the water quenching method, and the microstructure of the specimen before and after water quenching was observed. Furthermore, the temperature and thermal stress distributions in the specimen during instantaneous water quenching were simulated and analyzed in detail by finite element analysis.

3.1. Relative density and microstructure
While direct observation of exact mechanisms of such densification behavior is difficult, it seems that effects are primarily due to size difference between matrix phase and reinforcing phase (for ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites), and lubricating properties of CNTs (for ZrB2-CNT composites). These size difference and lubricating effects in composites seem to facilitate particle re-arrangement and better densification during stage I. The punch displacement during stage II decreases indicating thermal expansion of packed/locked powder mixtures. Finally, the punch displacement increases in early stage III indicating later stages of densification. The transition points between stages II and III correspond to punch displacement minima. For pure ZrB2, this stage II-III transition corresponds to starting of soaking stage ( t = 17 min; T = 1900 ◦ C). For ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples, this transition point shifts to left (earlier times) in the heating stage of the SPS sintering cycle (indicated by dotted line). For ZrB2-SiC (10-40% SiC) composite samples, this stage II-III transition point corresponds to about t = 11-15 min (corresponding temperature of ∼ 1300-1700 ◦ C in heating cycle). For ZrB2-CNT (2-6% CNT) composite samples, this stage II-III transition point corresponds to about t = 12-15 min (corresponding temperature of ∼ 1400-1700 ◦ C in heating cycle). This shift of stage II-III transition point indicates that final densification is favored at lower temperatures for ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples. The punch displacement increases in early stage III ( t = 18-19 min) without significant change during remaining stage III (soaking period corresponding to t > 20 min).

Besides, the geometry of the part and the starting powder characteristics strongly influence the evolved microstructure. For metal alloys,  the  heat  treatment,  temperature  gradients,  and  elevated  temperatures of the process can cause compositional changes due to solidstate  phase  transformations.  Since  EBS  only  melts  a  single-phase component powder (ZrSi2), it can be assumed that the effect of phase transformations is negligible. Also, significant evaporation might occur in alloys with elements susceptive to vaporization, resulting in components  with  a  much  different  composition  than  their  starting  powder [42].

1. Introduction
With the increased severity of hypersonic re-entry vehicles ' operating  conditions,  the  propulsion  system  requires  materials  that  can withstand  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C  [1].  Ceramic  matrix  composites (CMCs) such as C/C and C/SiC fibers are the existing materials of choice due  to  their  light-weight,  excellent  mechanical,  and  thermal  shock resistance [2]. However, carbon is susceptible to oxidation above 400 ◦ C and has poor stability in an erosive environment [3,4]. The addition of SiC only improves its stability until 1100 ◦ C due to the volatile nature of SiO2  at higher temperatures [5]. Due to the thermal limitations of C/C and  C/SiC,  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  considered good additives for conventional C/C composites. UHTCs are carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metal elements (group IV and V) with  a  combination  of  metal  and  ceramic-like  properties  that  offers excellent  stability  at  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C.  UHTC  is  a  potential candidate for a thermal protection system (TPS) for reusable re-entry vehicles. TPS  is necessary for spacecraft that enter the earth ' s

1. Introduction
Carbon /uniFB01 ber reinforced silicon carbide (C/SiC) composites are considered as potential candidate materials for high temperature structural components due to their low density, high strength, excellent corrosion resistance and good fracture toughness [1 e 3]. However, at higher temperatures ( > 1200 /C14 C), the oxidation behavior of the C/SiC composites is cooperatively in /uniFB02 uenced by the oxidation of the /uniFB01 bers, inter-phase and matrix [4,5]. Consequently, C/SiC composites require anti-oxidation protection when used at a higher temperature environment.
In the family of Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), two of the most interesting materials for thermal shielding applications are zirconium diboride (ZrB2) and zirconium carbide (ZrC), due to their extremely high melting temperatures, high hardness, and high retained strength at high temperature [6]. ZrB2 e SiC and ZrC e SiC ceramics have been proved as effective coatings for protecting carbon materials at high temperature [5 e 8].
The anti-oxidation ability of coatings is commonly tested at an invariable temperature [4 e 8]. However, most of space vehicles have to work in low-earth orbit environment, such as thermal shock, high vacuum (10 /C0 7 Pa), atomic oxygen, and so on. Vacuum is

3.3. Compressive behavior of porous YB2C2  samples
Finally,  benchmarking  against  other  porous  UHTCs,  as  shown  in Fig. 15 and Table 5, highlights the competitiveness of porous YB2C2. The expected decline of compressive strength with porosity is observed, yet our  PEP  plus in-situ boro/carbothermal reduction sintering  route  delivers  substantially  higher  strengths  at  comparable  porosities.  For example, at 57 -75 % porosity, the strengths reported by Chen et al. for partially  sintered  porous  YB2C2 fall  in  the  34.78 -9.32 MPa  range, whereas the present samples exceed these values over the same porosity window [38]. These results underscore the efficacy of the single-material, single-step PEP strategy in building continuous, long-range lamellae that elevate the compressive performance of porous ceramics.

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based composites have an extremely high melting point (>3000 /C176 C), high thermal and electrical conductivities, low density, and excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1-4]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramic composites have been considered as potential candidates for application in critical thermal protection systems and other components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles [1-7]. Silicon carbide (SiC) is commonly used as an additive to improve the oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC particulate composites (ZS) are regarded as the baseline material in this research. However, unsatisfactory thermal shock resistance remains the obstacle to the development of ZrB2-SiC ceramic on an industrial scale. To avoid or mitigate catastrophic failure of ZrB2SiC ceramics during thermal shock, most current researches focus on composition design to improve the performance by selecting different additives [8-12]. Carbon is also sometimes used as an additive to enhance thermal shock resistance [12-14]. Recently, Levine et al. [13] studied the mechanical properties of ZrB214 vol% SiC-30 vol% C. They concluded that the addition of carbon could reduce strength but increases fracture toughness. Hong et al. [14] fabricated ZrB2-SiC-graphite composite, which exhibits increased thermal shock resistance compared with monolithic ZrB2-SiC. The toughness and hence the reliability and thermal

1. Introduction
Porous ceramics with engineered anisotropy have attracted significant  attention  for  their  unique  combination  of  low  density,  tailored permeability, and direction-dependent mechanical and thermal properties, which are critical for advanced aerospace, energy, and environmental  applications  [1 -3].  Among  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics (UHTCs),  YB2C2  has  emerged  as  a  promising  candidate  owing  to  its exceptional thermal stability above 2500 ◦ C, excellent chemical inertness,  and  remarkable  resistance  to  thermal  shock  and  mechanical damage  [4].  For  instance,  YB2C2 retains  more  than  70 %  of  its room-temperature bending strength at 1300 ◦ C, and has demonstrated excellent chemical compatibility and enhanced mechanical performance

2.2. Characterization
Thermo  gravimetric  and  differential  scanning  calorimetry  experiments were carried out on a thermal analyzer (TG-DSC, STA 449F5, Netzsch, Germany) to determine the thermal debinding temperature of the polymer binders according to the weight loss. The solvent debinding green bodies approximately 2 mg were heated from room temperature to 800 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min under a flowing air atmosphere. The chemical and structural compositions of green bodies and brown bodies were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Germany). The phase composition of porous YB2C2  ceramics was analyzed using an X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advanced, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu K α radiation. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Merlin Compact, Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an energy dispersive  spectroscopy  (EDS)  detector  was  used  to  observe  the morphology of the samples. Compression tests  were performed  on  a universal  testing  machine  (UTM-50kN,  Zhejiang  Dana  Automation Technology Co. LTD., China). The average maximum load at failure of five  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  with  the  size  of  10 × 10 × 10 mm 3 was recorded as the compressive strength under a uniaxial compressive load at a crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min. The grain structure and crystallographic  texture  orientation  maps  of  the  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  in different directions (the X-Y surface and the X-Z surface) were determined using Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD, Oxford Symmetry 22, Voltage 20 kV, diaphragm 120 μ m). Samples for EBSD observation were subjected to standard metallographic procedures including cutting,  grinding  and  polishing.  Laser  confocal  microscopy  (VK-1000, Japan) was used to test the roughness of the samples, and DIC was used to test changes in the mechanical behavior of the samples.

3.3. Cycle dependence of thermal shock resistance
Fig. 7 shows the /uniFB02 exural strength of the C/C-ZrC composites experiencing di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles from 1700 °C to the room temperature. The /uniFB02 exural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shock cycles. The residual strength of the composite thermally shocked for 20 cycles is about 130 MPa, 70.3% of the as-produced C/C-ZrC composite. Mass change of the C/C-ZrC composite with di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles was also measured and listed in Fig. 7. The mass loss keeps constant without obvious di /uniFB00 erence, which is the same as the composite thermally shocked at di /uniFB00 erent temperatures (< 1900 °C).
Fig. 7. Flexural strength and mass loss of the C/C-ZrC composites subjected to di /uniFB00 erent thermal shock cycles.
Fig. 8 shows the cross section microstructure of the C/C-ZrC composite thermally shocked from 1700 °C to the room temperature for di /uniFB00 erent thermal cycles. The formation of matrix cracks and interfacial debonding can be clearly observed as that shown in Fig. 5. With increasing numbers of thermal cycles, the interfacial debonding becomes more severe. The cross section microstructure of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent numbers is dense without formation of pores, which is consistent with the mass constant listed in Fig. 7. Here, the shocked temperature is merely 1700 °C, which is not high enough to enable the evaporation of the Zr2Si phase. Thus, no obvious weight change was measured.

4. Conclusions
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) for PIP-C/SiC composites are deposited by slurry and CVD method, then the thermal shock behavior of the coated samples is studied, and the effect of thermal cycling on their /uniFB02 exural property and microstructures is discussed. The following conclusions have been reached:
- (1) ZrB2 e SiC (ZrC e SiC) coating is fabricated by slurry and CVD method with a thickness of 50 m m.
- (2) The coated samples showed the degradation of the /uniFB02 exural strength properties and no mass variation. Compared with the as-received C/SiC composites, the mode of the fracture behavior of coated C/SiC composites was changed, the samples exhibited an obvious brittle fracture behavior, and the coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.
- (3) ZrB2 e SiC coated C/SiC composites are no longer a singlephase of ZrB2, after thermal cycling tests, SiC and C are detected. The surface of ZrC e SiC coated C/SiC composites is no longer a single-phase of ZrC, SiC and C are also detected either.
- (4) The damage of the UHTCs based coatings caused by tensile stress could in /uniFB02 uence the mechanical properties of PIP-C/SiC composites under thermal cycling tests, and the /uniFB02 exural property of C/SiC composites is reduced.

3.2. Mechanical properties of the composites
Analogous behavior is also observed in the tensile strength of the C/ C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC  composites,  and  the  increase  is  more  obvious.  For example, starting with the C/C composites with the density of 1.00 g/ cm 3 (C/C-10),  after  4  and  7  PIP  cycles,  the  increase  of  the  tensile strength of the composites CZZS-1014 and CZZS-1016 after RMI process is  4.76%  and  9.38%,  respectively  (Table  1  and  Table  2),  which  is significantly higher than that of the composites CZS-10 (-13.64%) with 0 PIP cycles. For the composites with density of 1.20 g/cm 3 (C/C-12), after  2  and  9  PIP  cycles,  the  increase  of  the  tensile  strength  of  the composites CZZS-1214 and CZZS-1218 after RMI process is 20.83% and 56.76%, respectively (Table 1 and Table 2), which is also much higher than that of the composites CZS-12 (4.55%) with 0 PIP cycles. Therefore, the increase of both flexural and tensile strength of the composites after RMI can be strongly improved and optimized by tailoring the thickness of ZiC via PIP cycles and achieve 50.54% and 56.76% after 7 and 9 PIP cycles, respectively.
Due to the introduction of the ZiC, which can protect carbon fibers well  during RMI, thus the prepared C/C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC composites also shows improved mechanical properties compared to most of the composites prepared by similar processes (Table 3).

a b s t r a c t
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) are prepared on the surface of PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, and the thermal stability is one of the key issues for C/SiC composites. This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

3.4. Composite mechanical behaviour
The composite (Cf-YB2C2) was machined into bars and tested under bending to evaluate the strength at room temperature. Compared to a reference material based on a ZrB2/SiC matrix reinforced with continuous carbon fibres (Cf-ZrB2/SiC) with similar carbon fibre content and porosity, the ycBC-Cf composite yielded higher values of strength, which were only matched and surpassed in the case of the Y2O3 doped ZrB2/SiC material, where the YB2C2 is formed during sintering within the ZrB2/ SiC matrix. This highlights the promising mechanical properties of the YB2C2 phase combined with the fibre reinforcement, and confirms the synergistic effect in the ZrB2/SiC/Y2O3 matrix which leads to a decrease of porosity and an increase of the mechanical properties.
From  the  load/displacement  curves  of  the  Cf-YB2C2 composite (Fig.  11  a),  the  behaviour  observed  is  typical  of  other  UHTCMCs previously reported, mostly constituted by an elastic zone, followed by the  progressive  breaking  of  the  layers.  The  fracture  surface  appears rough and complex (Fig. 11 b), and looking at the details of the fractured matrix (Fig. 11 c), fibres appear well anchored to the ceramic matrix and the fibre pull-out is very limited, implying a strong fibre/matrix interface; moreover, the individual lamellae can be seen at different angles in spite of the partial anisotropy imparted by the applied pressure during sintering.  From  Fig.  11  d,  the  individual  YB2C2  lamellae  themselves showed some sliding over each other and some degree of lamellae pullout, which likely contributed to the high strength reported.

A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.

However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.

thermal stability, making it suitable for use at temperatures of 1600 ◦ C. 2-4 The ZrB2's impressive resistance to high-temperature structural degradation is attributed to the in situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO 2 -rich oxide scale, which provides additional thermal insulation and enhances its overall performance. 4-6 Incorporating silicon carbide (SiC) as reinforcement into the ZrB 2 matrix enhances its mechanical properties, oxidation, and ablation resistance. 6,7 Furthermore, the SiC reinforcement also inhibits ZrB2 grain growth during sintering, leading to enhanced densification. 3.1. SiC whisker reinforcement
Another emerging trend regarding whisker reinforced UHTCMCs is laminated  architectures.  Specifically,  set  of  recent  studies  [119,120] have  investigated  the  effect  of  having  porous  UHTC -SiCw  interfaces between  monolithic  UHTC  layers.  Weak  interfaces  such  as  BN  and graphite  have  previously  been  applied  to  induce  crack  deflection  in these lamented structures [121,122]. However, both BN and graphite are prone to oxidation at lower than desirable temperatures ( ≤ 800 ◦ C). Alternatively, a dense SiC layer has the potential to manifest as an undesirably strong interface. A layer composed of a UHTC ceramic with a high  volume  fraction  of  whiskers  could  instead  produce  a  relatively porous  interface  that  can  concomitantly  resist  oxidation  and  induce toughening mechanisms. This was achieved using whiskers of about 18 μ m length and 1.5 μ m diameter. The fabricated architecture is shown in Fig. 5a, where crack deflection and branching are apparent at the SiCw-UHTC  interface.  Fig.  5b  and  c  further  illustrate  how  microscale whisker  pullout  and  bridging  can  enforce  the  macroscale  crack  deflections. The reported flexure strengths were similar to values reported M. Ammendola et al.

The higher hardness for the ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 seems to be due to combination of effects such as higher relative density and presence of high volume fraction of harder reinforcement (SiC) particles. Sintering occurred at 2000 °C for 3 h. This research revealed that SiC content prevents grain growth, while changing such mechanical properties as /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness to 492 MPa from 404 and to 3.5 MPa m 1/2 from 3, respectively.
Emami et al. [28] produced ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC composites through mechanical activation spark plasma synthesis (MASPSyn) process. For this purpose, selected SiC content was about 31.6 vol% and ZrC content varied from 7.5 to 19.5 vol% and what remained was balanced by ZrB2 as the matrix. The best results for /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness were obtained for 15 vol% of ZrC content by measuring 620 MPa and 5.7 MPa m 1/2 , respectively.
Tsuchida et al. [58] used MA-SHS process to obtain ZrB2 and ZrC from elemental raw materials to produce ZrB2 -ZrC composites through the SPS process. The mechanical characteristics of the composites, estimated by Vickers indentation technique, showed the fracture toughness of 2.9 -5.1 MPa m 1/2 and Vickers hardness of 13.6 -17.8 GPa, depending on the molar ratio of ZrB2/ZrC.
Shahedi Asl et al. [43] investigated the hardness and fracture toughness of ZrB2 -20 vol% SiC -10 vol% Cf composite prepared by the SPS route. They revealed that the optimal attainable properties
