3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
oxidation of Ta over Nb is seen in all three samples has been attributed to two reasons: (i) Ta2O5 is more stable than Nb2O5, and (ii) Nb is present in  the  lower  content in  the pre-alloyed (Ta, Nb)C (80:20). Similarly, among the two Hf-containing samples, the content of HfO2 in ternary is more than quaternary is due to the high content of HfC in (50 vol%) in ternary,  which  also  led  to  the  segregation  of  the  phase  along  with complete solid-solution phase in the sample [7]. The content of unary oxide phases reduced from 82.7% in the binary compound to 21.5% in ternary and 12.9% in the quaternary compound. Hf-Ta oxide complex phases follow most Ti-Ta-rich oxide at the surface in quaternary UHTC while Hf-Ta complex oxide phase in the ternary system and unary oxide of Ta-Nb in binary UHTC. The majority of the oxide phase in the quaternary  system  is  rich  in  Ti-based  oxide.  This  could  be  due  to  the segregation of the TiC phase (up to 3 wt%, see Fig. 3) near the grain boundaries, which forms additional complex mixed oxide of Ti during plasma oxidation. Also, Ti is the smallest, lightest, and oxidation-prone among  the  metallic  elements  of  (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C  [12].  It  is  worth mentioning that the high-resolution SEM on this sample reported in the recent study by authors could not detect TiC segregation [7] due to the smaller amount of undissolved TiC (~3 wt%) in the sample. EBSD, a more precise technique, could visualize the distribution of phases and quantify it qualitatively (see Fig. 3).

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
sharp leading edges in re-entry vehicles. These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Fig. 5 e-g shows the high-resolution elemental scan for Ta, Hf, Nb, and  Ti  metal  cations  in  quaternary  UHTC  system.  The  peaks  corresponding to Ta, Hf, and Nb were similar to those obtained in the ternary UHTC system, albeit with slight shift ( < 5%) due to the addition of TiC in the quaternary system. Similar to ternary UHTC, two additional minor peaks of Ta4f7/2 and Ta4f5/2 with binding energies 24.4 eV, and 26.2 eV, respectively, correspond to the carbide peak of Ta with a shift towards the higher energy due to the solid-solution formation in a four component system. The shift in the M GLYPH<0> C towards higher energy could be due to the formation of oxy-carbide. The substitution of C by O in the M GLYPH<0> C bond leading to the formation of oxy-carbide has shown to have a higher binding energy of 1 -2 eV [28]. The intensity ratio of Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 is constrained by 1:2, which is maintained in the present case. As per the literature, Ti peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 453.74 eV, and 459.79 eV while Ti(IV) oxide (TiO2) peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 458.59 and 464.31 eV [46]. The observed peak position of  Ti2p,  which  corresponds  to  2p3/2,  is  at  458.8  eV,  confirming  the presence of the rutile TiO2 phase and a shoulder peak at 457.32 eV. This shoulder peak can be due to Ti ' s other complex mixed oxide phases, as observed in the XRD analysis (see Fig. 2, Table 2). Hence, in the quaternary system, the major oxidation by-products comprises of two mixed complex oxide phases of Ta-Ti-O and Hf-Ta-O (and not TiO2  alone), analogous  to  the  results  obtained  from  Rietveld  analysis  (Fig.  2, Table 2).

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Table 3 summarizes the Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, Hf4f, O1s, and C1s peaks from XPS spectra collected for the plasma exposed binary, ternary, and quaternary UHTCs along with the literature peaks for carbides and oxides. The relative intensity of elements ' peaks in XPS spectra can provide insight into oxidation thermodynamic and kinetic theories. This can be well  understood by comparing the relative intensities of various elements in a particular sample. The Gibbs free energy of oxide formation for Group V (Ta,Nb) is lower than that of Group IV (Hf,Ti), other things being equal [28]. Hence, the preferential oxide growth of Ti and Hf are more favorable once the oxidation process is activated [26], as observed from the XRD analysis (see Table 2). The virtual crystal approximation was used to model the carbon vacancies and the calculations  qualitatively  demonstrated  that  vanadium  strongly  favored segregation to the boride phase when VC was carbon deficient (i.e., VCx where x is less than 1), which might be the case for the carbide formed in the HEDP-V. If the VC were fully stoichiometric (i.e., x = 1), vanadium segregation  to  the  carbide  may  have  been  favored.  Results  from  the present  study  reveal  that  first-principles  simulations  can  predict  the metal distributions based solely on thermodynamics, but complex interactions that also dictate phase distribution of the metals need further investigation.
The heat map analysis of the HEDP-Nb and -Ta compositions also exhibited  variation  in  intensity,  but  the  segregation  order  remained consistent across both compositions: Ti < Zr < Nb < Hf and Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta from boride to carbide. As an example, for HEDP-Ta, the boride/ carbide  ratios  were  Ti < Zr < Hf < Ta,  experimentally,  these  values were 2.35 > 0.94 > 0.67 > 0.61, while theoretically, they were 8.25 > 2.84 > 0.44 > 0.07. This discrepancy arises because thermodynamic calculations did not predict the absolute concentration of each metal  in  each  phase  [13],  as  it  is  dependent  on  their  ratio  between boride and carbide phases but rather indicated the relative favorability of metal segregation between phases.

3.1. Oxide layer formation
Preand post-oxidation analysis of all the composites, show that all the samples (TaC, T15S, T15C and T15S15C) survived the plasma jet flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 for 30 s, see Fig. 3a, and no appreciable erosion was observed on the front face of the TaC-SiC-CNT samples. Apparently, surface was without cracks on all TaC based composites, and the formed oxide scale was adherent to the base material (see Fig. 3a). The back face rise in temperature for all plasma arc tested UHTC composites (shown in Fig. 3b) is observed to decrease with SiC addition and increases with CNT reinforcement in the TaC matrix.
Qualitative changes occurring during oxidation of TaC-based composites are clearly evident from the subsequent appearance of orthorhombic Ta2O5 peaks obtained in XRD spectra (Fig. 4a). However, retention of TaC phase were also observed and the amount of TaC phase was quantified on the surface of exposed samples as an average of area fraction of TaC under XRD peaks (see Table 1). The crystallite size ( t ), evaluated using Scherrer's formula (Eq. (3)) for all as-prepared samples, are of similar order ( ∼ 25-31 nm), however the crystallite size marginally increased after plasma arc jet exposure ( ∼ 40-51 nm), shown in Table 1. It is elucidated that the formation of surface oxide was suppressed with SiC addition as the amount of retained TaC phase was increased from ∼ 25% (for TaC) to 36% (T15S), but decreases with CNT addition to 18% (for T15C) and 22% (T15S15C). CNT addition protects TaC from oxidation as explained in the following section. The formation of other oxides (TaO2 and Ta2O3) were not observed after plasma arc jet exposure, though they have been reported at intermediate temperatures of around 900-1300 ◦ C, depending on the oxygen partial pressure [42].

A B S T R A C T
complex mixed oxide structures have a significant impact on inhibiting oxygen transport. A systematic study comparing different compositions of TaC-HfC solid solution (with varying vol% of HfC from 0, 20, 50, 80 to 100 ) shows remarkable improvement in the thermo-mechanical properties [17]. The solid solution approach improved the oxidation resistance  of  unary  UHTCs  up  to  90%  due  to  the  complex  mixed  oxide formation, Hf6Ta2O17 [17]. Negligible oxidation of MC-UHTCs i.e. (Ta, Nb,Hf)C and (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C has also been attributed to Hf6Ta2O17 formation  as  one  of  the  major  phases  during  plasma  exposure  [9,16]. Hence, understanding the complex oxide system should be of interest to comprehend  the  improved  oxidation  resistance  of  solid-solution  and MC-UHTCs.

[10] showed oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-graphene nanoplatelets (1 to 5 vol%) (GNP) composites processed at temperature of 1850 ◦ C under high temperature plasma flow of 30 kW power for 60 s and showed that addition of GNP suppresses formation of oxide layer up to 60%. However, high temperature thermal conductivity for TaC based composites is not available in literature, which is one of the important attributes to survive high temperature exposure.
Improvement of fracture toughness with SiC and CNT addition in TaC-based UHTCs is demonstrated elsewhere [39]. Hence, the scope of the present work is to develop an understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of TaC-SiC-CNT composites to predict the lifetime performance of UHTCs under operational conditions. Oxidation behaviour is analysed in terms of the oxide layer formation during testing. To supplement plasma arc jet testing, thermogravimetric analysis is carried out for 30 min. at 1500 ◦ C in oxygen environment. Thermal conductivity of these materials have been measured using laser flash diffusivity technique and, accordingly, correlation of the heat transfer mechanism during plasma arc jet testing is established. An attempt has been made to associate the synergistic effect of SiC and CNT reinforcement on the performance of TaC-based UHTCs through these investigations.

3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
To determine the exact amount of phases present on the surface of each sample after plasma arc jet exposure, Rietveld refinement is performed over the XRD pattern, presented in Fig. 2. It is evident from Fig. 2 that  the  major  oxide  peaks  correspond  to  the  simple  oxide  phase  of Ta2O5 in a binary UHTC, which then changes to complex mixed oxides of (Hf-Ta) and (Ti-Ta), respectively in ternary and quaternary UHTC systems. The percentage of various phases present in the oxidized samples is tabulated in Table 2. The phases on the oxide scale have been quantified  based  on  the  prospective  oxide  phases  mentioned  in  Table  1. However, insufficient thermodynamic data on complex oxide in the case of a ternary and quaternary system limits their exact quantification. The overlap in the peak position of the multiple oxide phases in the XRD pattern  of  the  surface  oxide  layer  in  each  sample  makes  the  exact quantification of phases arduous. However, the XRD pattern was only fitted with the phases considering the peak intensity, and its position as a good match.
The XRD pattern on the plasma exposed sample surface shows the complete oxidation of the solid-solution phase in binary UHTC system while it was retained in MC-UHTC systems. It is also evident from the thicker oxide scale seen in Fig. 1 a . It is also to be mentioned here that the solid solution phase still exists in the unaffected region of the binary UHTC system.

question: How do multi-component UHTCs containing molybdenum carbide (MoC) components compare to group IV and V element-based UHTCs in terms of preferential oxidation and complex oxide layer formation after plasma jet exposure?
answer: The study indicates that the addition of group VI elements, such as molybdenum (Mo), to group IV base materials is preferable over group V additions for improving oxidation resistance, especially under hypersonic flight conditions [4]. The presence of group VI oxides in the thermally grown scale shows that porosity due to volatile oxides is observed on the surface of the scale, but the mode of attack was more uniform, despite evidence of preferential oxidation [4]. Additionally, any detrimental effects due to the non-group IV elements present in the oxide phase, in this case Mo and W, were minimized due to (a) their oxides having limited solubility in group IV oxides, being volatile and leaving the system, and (b) their preferential retention as a carbide phase [4]. Thus, the group IV + VI elemental combination is a promising route for the design of HE-UHTCs [4].

This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).

These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
Based on the limited available data in the literature [13,30,46,58], a comparison  of  the  mechanical  and  oxidation  resistance  of  mono, binary,  ternary,  and  multi-component  UHTC  carbides  (referred  to  as HE-carbide) is summarized and plotted in Fig. 7. The improvement in the hardness (see Fig. 7) has mainly been attributed to the solid-solutioning in the binary, ternary, and HE-UHTCs. The onset temperature at which the oxide starts forming has remarkably improved from 900 °C in monolith UHTCs to > 1100 °C for HE-UHTCs (Fig. 7 ) . This is possibly due to the formation of the complex, layered structure as well as the composition of the oxide scale in a multi-component system. The innermost oxide layer near the unoxidized sample usually has a dense structure with lesser pores (all filled with molten particles) followed by a porous oxide structure with lesser pores (see Fig. 5). Also, the complex composition of the mixed oxides due to multi-constituents restricts the inward  diffusion  of  O2 providing  oxidation  resistance,  discussed  in section 3.2. However, whether all components in a solid-solution of the HE-UHTC  system  react  with  oxygen  simultaneously  or  preferential oxidation, still needs to be looked upon in both isothermal and highspeed  plasma  jet  conditions.  Also,  this  brings  up  a  question  of  how many  constituents  (four,  five,  six,  or  more)  in  a  multi-component system would be sufficient for enhancement in the properties in the absence of entropy quantification. To get an insight into it, a study on gradually raising the constituents in UHTCs from mono, binary and so on is required along with the delineation of the entropy contributions to the properties.

4.3. Design of oxidation-resistant HE-UHTCs
as Hf6Ta2O17, which may promote oxidation resistance. However, while there is evidence for the presence of such complex oxides, the results discussed here and for the oxidation of (HfZrTiTaNb)C [9], as well as in a more recent HfC-TaC oxidation study [11], show that excess Ta additions in materials that oxidize intergranularly (such as UHTC carbides) result in liquid oxide formation, which in turn promotes ' premature ' densification of the intergranular oxides (before an external scale can form). In the cases of Ta additions, oxygen transport towards the center of the grains in the oxidation-affected zone is slowed due to the densified intergranular oxide, while intergranular oxygen transport continues to occur into the unoxidized material.
The presence of group VI oxides in the thermally grown scale show that porosity due to volatile oxides is observed on the surface of the scale (Figs. 13 and 15), but the mode of attack was more uniform, despite evidence of preferential oxidation. The pores observed were just below the surface and smaller than that seen in the cross sections of group V containing compositions (e.g., Fig. 16). Any detrimental effects due to the non-group IV elements present in the oxide phase, in this case Mo and W, was minimized due to (a) their oxides having limited solubility in group IV oxides, being volatile and leaving the system, and (b) their preferential retention as a carbide phase. The material consumption data confirms that at the conditions tested, group VI additions to group IV base-material are preferable over group V additions. This is especially true for conditions that likely prevail during hypersonic flight. Thus, the group IV + VI elemental combination is a promising route for the design of HE-UHTCs.

Applied Surface Science
A companion  publication [7]  revealed  that  MC-UHTCs  have remarkable oxidation resistance compared to unary and binary UHTC attributed to mixed complex oxide phase formation with Hf6Ta2O17 as a major phase. The present work focuses on qualitatively and quantitatively investigating the content and chemical composition of the oxide scale in the binary and MC-UHTCs using core level spectroscopy correlated with phase quantification via Rietveld analysis. Based on the review of various available phase diagrams of a binary and ternary system, the possible combination of oxide phases in the chosen chemistry for the present work is summarized in Table 1.
Fig. 2. XRD pattern of the surface oxide layer formed on the plasma exposed binary and MC-UHTC systems. The colored dots in each XRD pattern represents the experimental raw data, while the lines denote calculated data after Rietveld refinement.
The present study explains how preferential oxidation based on the components added in the UHTC system can improve or deteriorate its oxidation resistance. These findings can enable oxidation-resistant designs for non-oxide multi-component UHTCs.

3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure
Further,  the  cross-sectional  SEM  of  samples  was  conducted  to quantify the thickness of the oxide film formation on the sample surface due to the melting of the Ta2O5  phase, see Fig. 9c and d. The minor cracking in the 50HO-50TO sample vs. wide crack opening in the 70HO30TO sample in the unaffected region elicits that the intense plasma exposure was enough to create thermal stresses, analogous to the strain analysis in Table 1. The thickness of melted and then resolidified oxide film is 210 nm and 297 nm in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Although there is no significant change in the thickness of melted oxide in both the samples, there are significantly more apparent melted regions in the 70HO-30TO sample. Hence, the 50HO-50TO sample has better  thermal  stability  than  the  70HO-30TO  sample.  In  terms  of improving the oxidation resistance of solid-solution binary UHTCs or MC-UHTCs, it can be anticipated that a composition with equal or higher content of TaC will have a higher probability of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during plasma exposure. This complex mixed oxide phase act as a protective layer that suppresses further oxidation. Also, the protective layer in UHTC components is formed due to the preferential oxidation of various  components  at  service  temperature  [16].  The  present  study shows that the complex mixed oxide formation during service ( > 2500 ◦ C) of solid-solution binary UHTCs and MC-UHTCs improves its oxidation resistance due to the thermal stability and mechanical integrity of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase. UHTC composition, thus designed to form a protective and thermally stable Hf6Ta2O17 phase under extreme conditions, will provide new directions to the materials for reusable hypersonic applications.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have shown aspiration to overcome challenges in the thermal protection system (TPS) by designing new materials referred to as multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) in the compositional space. MC-UHTCs have shown remarkable improvement in oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure during plasma exposure. Herein, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure is synthesized via a solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 powder mixtures during spark plasma sintering (SPS). The compositions chosen are 50 vol% of HfO2 -50 vol% of Ta2O5 (50HO-50TO) and 70 vol% of HfO2 -30 vol% of Ta2O5 (70HO-30TO). The phase quantification via Rietveld analysis showed Hf6Ta2O17 as a principal phase with some residual Ta2O5  phase in both the samples. The high-temperature thermal stability of the samples was evaluated using high-velocity plasma jet exposure for up to 3 min. 50HO-50TO was able to withstand the intense plasma condition, which is attributed to the higher content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase (~84%) and lower strain in the Ta2O5 phase. The augmentation in the Hf6Ta2O17 phase to 94.7% (in 50HO-50TO) post plasma exposure has been attributed to the invariant transformation from a liquid state to Hf6Ta2O17 at temperatures > 2500 ◦ C during testing. The mechanical integrity is elucidated from the insignificant change in the hardness ~13.3 GPa before and 11.2 GPa after plasma exposure of the 50HO-50TO sample. As a result, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure ' s thermo-mechanical stability suggests developing novel oxidation-resistant MC-UHTCs in compositional space for reusable space vehicle applications.

4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

3.1. Oxide layer formation
Preand post-oxidation analysis of all the composites, show that all the samples (TaC, T15S, T15C and T15S15C) survived the plasma jet flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 for 30 s, see Fig. 3a, and no appreciable erosion was observed on the front face of the TaC-SiC-CNT samples. Apparently, surface was without cracks on all TaC based composites, and the formed oxide scale was adherent to the base material (see Fig. 3a). The back face rise in temperature for all plasma arc tested UHTC composites (shown in Fig. 3b) is observed to decrease with SiC addition and increases with CNT reinforcement in the TaC matrix.
Qualitative changes occurring during oxidation of TaC-based composites are clearly evident from the subsequent appearance of orthorhombic Ta2O5 peaks obtained in XRD spectra (Fig. 4a). However, retention of TaC phase were also observed and the amount of TaC phase was quantified on the surface of exposed samples as an average of area fraction of TaC under XRD peaks (see Table 1). The crystallite size ( t ), evaluated using Scherrer's formula (Eq. (3)) for all as-prepared samples, are of similar order ( ∼ 25-31 nm), however the crystallite size marginally increased after plasma arc jet exposure ( ∼ 40-51 nm), shown in Table 1. It is elucidated that the formation of surface oxide was suppressed with SiC addition as the amount of retained TaC phase was increased from ∼ 25% (for TaC) to 36% (T15S), but decreases with CNT addition to 18% (for T15C) and 22% (T15S15C). CNT addition protects TaC from oxidation as explained in the following section. The formation of other oxides (TaO2 and Ta2O3) were not observed after plasma arc jet exposure, though they have been reported at intermediate temperatures of around 900-1300 ◦ C, depending on the oxygen partial pressure [42].

Hence, the preferential oxide growth of Ti and Hf are more favorable once the oxidation process is activated [26], as observed from the XRD analysis (see Table 2). 1.1. Expected oxidation behavior of group IV compounds with group V and VI additions
Group V additions to group IV ceramics in various forms, mainly as Ta compounds, have resulted in some limited improvement in oxidation behavior, whether through liquid phase assisted sintering of the group IV oxides, the doping of group IV oxides with higher valence ions to limit oxygen transport through oxygen vacancies [13], or the formation of ordered compounds such as A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb) [13,22] (Fig. 1(a)). On the other hand, liquid oxide formation at more aggressive conditions  and  ultra-high  temperatures  has  been  shown  to  be  detrimental to the oxidation behavior of UHTCs. Results discussed in previous work [9] for the group IV + V combination shows that the oxidation behavior of the solid solution closely matches the design considerations [5] from the constituent elements, i.e., the formation of group V oxides, for example, promoted the formation of oxides with low melting temperatures  that  resulted  in  a  decrease  in  oxidation  resistance  of  the material.

A B S T R A C T
High entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics (HE-UHTCs) have garnered intense research interest due to the potential for optimized oxidation and mechanical properties for extreme environment applications. HE-UHTCs are  expected to oxidize according to the thermodynamic favorability of their respective oxidation reactions, which varies according to the periodic grouping. Based on this, the oxidation resistance of equimolar (metalsbasis) group IV + V (HfZrTiTaNb), group IV + V + VI (HfZrTiTaMo) and group IV + VI (HfZrTiMoW) carbides and borides were evaluated at 1700 ◦ C in 1 mol% O2  for 5 min and compared. Group IV elements oxidized preferentially in all three compositions. Group V element-containing carbides exhibited the lowest oxidation resistance, attributed to the formation of intergranular liquid oxides. (HfZrTiMoW)C exhibited the best resistance among the carbides. The diborides exhibited similar material consumption, reinforcing the hypothesis that the oxidation behavior under these conditions is controlled by the presence of boria. These findings provide direction for HE-UHTC composition design for oxidation resistance.

Additionally, CrB2 additions were found to be less effective than Ta or Nb-based additions in improving oxidation resistance [6]. Vanadium oxides have low melting temperatures 5 and form low-temperature melting eutectics with the oxides of the other elements under consideration, e.g. a eutectic in the ZrO2 -V2O5 is noted at 665 ◦ C [7], It is important to consider whether the thermodynamic considerations previously discussed [5,8] can be extended to compositions with group VI additions and determine whether they will oxidize to form volatile products. 2.2. Plasma jet exposure
The  oxidation  behavior  of  binary  and  MC-UHTCs  was  evaluated using a high-velocity plasma gun (Praxair SG-100 DC). The setup used for this study has been designed in-house, details of which can be found in our previous studies [7,29]. The plasma was generated using a plasma spray gun which is capable of simulating extreme re-entry conditions by generating temperatures > 10,000 ◦ C. SPS samples of 20 mm diameter and 3 -4 mm thickness were exposed for 3 mins to plasma generated with an input power of 30 kW. A K-type thermocouple was used to measure the back-side sample temperature throughout the testing. The samples were kept at a distance of 75 mm from the plasma gun to confirm the abundance of oxygen from the surrounding. The samples in the present study were  held with vacuum  instead  of mounting  with  hightemperature phenolic resin to avoid contamination and any alterations in the thermal stress distributions.
